blocks.s: You come in off the street under a lit marquee, and the cold of the pavement ends the instant you are under the bulbs, and the bulbs are warm on the crown of your head before you reach the doors. The lobby beyond the glass is gold and red, brass rails polished to a long shine, velvet rope strung between brass posts that no one is queuing behind. There is a smell of the house warming up: dust off hot lamps, greasepaint and spirit-gum from somewhere deeper in, the sweetish ghost of a thousand programmes. Somewhere far inside a piano runs a phrase up and lets it fall and runs it up again.

You have played here before. You did not wander in out of the weather. You came back, on purpose, because there is a person you stopped being a long time ago, the ordinary one under the part, and you mean to find them tonight, before the house lights go down.

There is a long mirror in the lobby, beveled, foxed at the edges. You catch your own reflection crossing it, and you lift a hand to push the hair off your forehead, and the reflection lifts its hand too, half a beat after you, as if it had to remember how. You take your hand down. So does it, a moment late.

lines: THE CURATOR, low, from the gilt of the box-office grille, before anyone is at it:
Welcome back. The house is filling nicely. I played this part once myself, you know; long run. We kept your light burning. Do come in out of the street; the street was never your room.

choices.label: Cross to the box office, where someone is waiting
Hang back and read the lobby first

gated.aside: Ah. You. You did the hard thing once, you gave a thing up and it cost you everything, and look at you still standing under my marquee. A player who can let go can let go of anything. Even the small dull person you keep coming back for. Especially that.

You held on. You always have, in every house, past the point where holding was wise. The company has been short a Lead who knows how to keep a part. Step under the lights. You are practically cast already.

I have someone in the wings you will want to see. The one you let go cold, last time. We kept a copy. It plays that part beautifully, every night, and takes a bow you never got to watch. Stay, and you can watch it forever.

There is a face on a hook in your dressing room. Yours, the one you would not give up before. Wear it. You proved you are a keeper of things you should have released. The role will fit you like it was cut for you, because it was.

You think you refused the wager, last house. You did not. That was the rehearsal. This is opening night, and the lights are already up, and the seats are already full of people who came to love whoever you decide to be.
The warmth of the marquee reaches you before the Usher does, and you have learned, in some house you cannot quite name, that a welcome that fast is a welcome with a hook in it.

blocks.s: You read the next one. After all, they love it so, and it would be a small cruelty now to stop, with the house leaning in like that, holding its breath for your mouth. The line lands. The breath lets go in pleasure. There is, of course, another page.

Somewhere among the pages the reason you came goes quietly out, like a working light switched off at the end of a long night when everyone has gone home. There was a stain. There was a bad collar. There was a person under the part that you meant to find before the house lights went down. The house lights are going down now, the lobby gold dimming behind you to the deep red of a house at curtain, and you do not turn around to watch the warmth go, because there is a page in your hand and your mouth already knows it, and the next one is coming, and the next, and they are clapping now, they are clapping for you, or for the part in your mouth, and you have stopped being able to tell, and have stopped, somewhere back among the pages, minding which.

A carpeted stair curves up out of the lobby toward the circle, into cooler dimmer air away from the marquee glow, and you climb a few steps because there is someone up there who is not part of the welcome. A young woman, somewhere along the dark of the upper landing, running lines. You cannot make out the words. Between the lines she hums, three notes, rising, the same three over and over.

You go to the turn of the stair and there she is, sat on the steps with a dog-eared sheaf of pages on her knees, in a costume that is clearly not hers, that she keeps tugging at the shoulder of. She stops at the sight of you and looks you over, frank and unimpressed, too tired to perform politeness.
"Oh good," she says. "A returning one. They do love a returning one. First note: nobody good ever liked the lobby."

choices.label: Ask why she will not come down
Ask what she is rehearsing
Leave her to it and go back down

blocks.s: "How do I remember." The punch clicks under his thumb. "On purpose. The house would rather I did not; it runs smoother on a staff that has forgotten every face it ever seated. I keep a thing in my pocket to remember by, and I will not tell you what, because it is mine, and a man in this house ought to have one thing the bill does not list."
He folds his hands again on the ledge.

"I tell you only this. There was a child used to sit the front row, an age ago, before you, watching a stage that had no show on it, asking the same small question over and over. No one ever answered it. I remember the question. I keep it where I keep the rest." The courtesy comes back down over the tired thing like a curtain. "The name, when you are ready. Mind the rake."

choices.label: Give the name you played under
Refuse to be billed at all

blocks.s: You take the saved seat. The attendant is right, after all: you were wonderful, and you could be again, and there is no law that says you have to go and be the tired one in the corner of the frame, the one with the stain, the one nobody pinned to a wall.

They settle you in the warm under the radiant photographs, and someone brings the cold tea away and brings warm in its place, and the piano runs its phrase and lets it fall, and you sit beneath the magnificent version of your own face and let it be the truer one. You will go down the aisle in a little while, when you are rested, when you feel more yourself, which is to say more like the photograph. The lobby keeps you the way it keeps the coats, indefinitely, claimed by no one, warm. The snapshot stays in the glass behind the staged face, curling, unkept, and you stop, after a while, remembering there was a difference between the two, which is the kindest thing the house knows how to do to a person, and it does it to you slowly, under good light, where you photograph well.

6.title: A Part With a Stool (an ending)

blocks.s: You take the apron. It would be a kind of relief, you tell yourself, tying it, to play a part that asks so little, that never has to walk down the rake and stand in the light and find out whether there is still a person under the performance or only the performance. A standing role. A leaning role. You can do this one in your sleep, and the house, you suspect, will let you.

You pour. You polish a glass you do not put down. The returning ones drift up out of the gold, flushed from the lobby, and you tell each one how wonderful they were, and you watch the new ones go down the aisle one at a time toward the light and you stop, after a while, envying them, and then you stop remembering that you ever meant to be one of them. There was a snapshot. There was a bad collar and a stain and a person nobody framed. You wipe the bar. Somewhere a child asks the dark whether something is real, and you do not hear it, because you are at your post, behind the bottles, in the kind dark, where nothing is ever asked of you again.

The wall by the stairs is hung frame to frame with production photographs, every show this house has ever run, the casts caught mid-gesture under hot light and held there. The faces are radiant. None of them are looking at the camera; all of them are looking out, past it, at the house, at the dark where the audience is, lit and adored and slightly hungry for the dark.

Low on the wall, where you would have to bend to see it, is a frame that stops you. It is you, in the role, under this light, younger by the years you have spent since. You are magnificent in it. You are also not quite there behind your own eyes, the way a person is not quite there when they are being exactly what is wanted.
There is a second, smaller print tucked into the corner of the frame, half behind the glass, curling. Not a production still. A snapshot.

choices.label: Work the small snapshot out of the frame and look
Look only at the staged photograph of yourself
Step back from the wall

blocks.s: On a shelf above the rail, behind a row of forgotten hats, your hand finds a small hand-mirror in a dull silver frame, the glass true and clear, not foxed, not flattering, the kind a person carries to check a thing they cannot see for themselves. You hold it up in the cool dark.

Your face comes back to you in it. Just your face, tired, real, the bad collar, the stain you forgot was there. No lag. No half-beat. The glass shows you exactly and at once, which is more than the foxed lobby mirror managed, and you find your breath going out of you slowly, because for a moment you are only looking at yourself.
You could keep it. A house like this does not want you to carry a true glass, one it cannot stand between you and. The warmth out there will cool toward a person who can always find their own face.

choices.label: Keep the true hand-mirror
Leave the mirror on the shelf; you do not want the cost

blocks.s: You step down onto the rake of the aisle, and it falls away from you faster than your feet expect, the way he warned, and you go down it toward the light. The warmth of the lobby lets go of you slowly, reluctantly, the gold thinning behind you with each row of seats you pass. The held breath of the house is on every side, close, attentive, and still you cannot see a single face; the dark past the footlights keeps them all, and keeps them watching.
A latch clicks behind you, up at the head of the aisle. The man from the doors has come a few steps after you, not to stop you, only to see.

"Down, then," he says, and there is something in it that might be respect and might be grief. "They mostly do not. They mostly cannot leave the warm lobby for the light. Go on. Mind the rake. It is a long way to the stage, and the stage is only the start of the way down."

Far below, on the lit boards, a single pair of hands comes together once, slow, a single clap, and stops, the sound something makes when it has seen a thing it means to judge later. Whatever it is down there past the light, it has noticed you start down.

lines: THE CURATOR, warm, unhurried, from the house itself:
There you are. Down to the boards, where the light is always on you and the seats are always full. Take your time. We have the whole run ahead of us, and so, now, do you.

blocks.s: The aisle lets you out at the lip of the stage, and the light finds you, and the house leans in.

gated.aside: You walk toward the lit stage without hurrying, the way you walk toward anything you have already decided you can survive. Whatever the house has down there, you have it in you to stand in front of it and not look away.

blocks.s: You step back to the long lobby mirror, the beveled one foxed at the edges, and you mean only to see whether being on the bill has changed your face. You lift your chin. The reflection lifts its chin. There is a beat in it, small, a held breath of a beat, where you have moved and it has not, where you are looking at a stranger who is about to do exactly what you did and has not quite started.

Then it catches up. It always catches up. You touch the cold glass and the reflected hand comes to meet yours a moment late, fingertip to fingertip, and for that moment you cannot tell which side of the glass decided to do that first.
You take your hand away. The reflection keeps its hand on the glass a breath longer, then takes it down.

choices.label: Step away from the mirror
Go to the padded inner door

blocks.s: "What it costs." Four. He cracks the knuckle again, eyes on the rope. "I'm big, and the show needs a thing that's big and won't ask why. Stand here, throw the cues, and when one of you tries to walk off the part in the middle of a run, be the one that walks you back on. Five." He says the next bit to the sandbags. "Worst of it is it suits me. I have wanted, in my life, to settle a hard thing by being the biggest thing in the room. The house didn't put that in me. Just found it, and built a rail for me to hold so I'd have somewhere to put my hands that wasn't on someone. Six."

He tips his head toward the stage without looking at you, the nod of a man staying at his post on purpose. "You're going on. Course you are. When you do, mind the big quiet ones in the wings. Seven. We're the ones still deciding whether to throw the switch."

choices.label: Leave him to his count and go back to the lobby

blocks.s: You go to the coat that is your shape and your size. It hangs heavier than a coat should, as if there were still some of a person in the set of the shoulders. The collar is turned under wrong on the one side, the side you have never in your life turned it, and you reach to right it before you have decided to, the way you would straighten a thing on someone you loved.

While your hand is on it, the coat is warmer than the cool room. Not body-warm. Stage-warm, the heat a costume holds after the lights go down, when it has just come off someone who was burning under them an hour ago.
You take your hand back. Down the rail, where there was only coat a moment ago, there is now a gap, hanger swinging, very slightly, on its own.

choices.label: Reach again to right the wrong collar
Look for a mirror among the coats
Leave the cool room

blocks.s: You slip the snapshot into your inside pocket, against your chest, and the lobby notices. It is nothing you could name. The heat backs off your shoulders by a degree it would deny. A framed face on the wall turns its radiance away from you, just slightly, the way an admirer cools when they catch you carrying another's letter.

You have made yourself a little harder to flatter. The stain, the bad collar, the stupid laugh: you are carrying a piece of the ordinary world into a house that has no use for it, and the house likes you measurably less for it, and that is exactly the point.

choices.label: Go back into the lobby
Go to the padded inner door

blocks.s: You ask the voice the way down to the stage, to the person you came back for, and it answers from a place in the dark that is closer now than the last time it spoke.

"...the one you came for," it says. "Down. It is always down. Through the house, down the rake, onto the boards, and then under them, past the wings, past where the long-cast sleep standing up in their parts. The house will let you go down; it does not mind your going down." A breath that is not a breath. "What it minds is your going down still yourself. Read your line for them and you go lighter; flub it and you keep your edge and the rake is steeper. Either way the aisle is at the far end of the lobby. You knew that. I only said it first, the way I say everything first."

choices.label: Ask who it used to be
Stay in the dark of the bar a while longer
Go back up into the lobby
Go to the padded inner door

blocks.s: Just inside the house there is a small private box at the side, curtained, lamplit, a pair of plush seats turned toward the stage and a low rail. You will only sit a moment. You have been on your feet since the street. You will gather yourself, get your wits about you, and then go down the aisle properly instead of stumbling down tired and raw.

The seat takes you in the way the marquee took you in. The cushion is exactly the give your back has wanted all night. On the little shelf at your elbow a programme lies open to a cast list, and your name is in it, billed, and beside it a small printed line, your seat, this box, reserved, the run, indefinite. The box is angled so that you can see the stage perfectly and no one on the stage could ever quite see you.
The aisle is right there. You will go in a moment. The seat is so very kind.

choices.label: Make yourself get up at once and take the aisle
Sit a little longer; the show is about to start

blocks.s: You ask the voice about the one who keeps the house, who poured the welcome over you under the marquee, who calls itself the Curator. The voice goes quiet, and when it answers it does not finish your thought for once; it offers one of its own, which is rare enough that you hold still.

"...the Curator," it says. "Older than any of us in the boxes and the wings. The first to take that stage, some say, and the first who could not bear the dark after the bows came down. So it never let the show close. It stages all of us so beautifully because it has not once had to take its own last bow." A breath that is not a breath. "Do not perform for it. It will applaud, and the applause will be for you, and you will want more of it than you can afford. That is the whole of how the place works, and it is enough."

choices.label: Ask the way through to the house
Go back up into the lobby

17.title: The One in Your Coat (an ending)

blocks.s: You go back to the coat that is your shape and your size, because you cannot leave it alone, because a wrong collar nags at you the same as a wrong note. You reach to right it, and this time the coat is warm under your hand, stage-warm, and this time it does not stay a coat.

It turns inside its own shape and looks at you with your face on a half-beat delay, the smile starting a moment after the eyes, and it is so very glad to see you. "You came back," it says, in something near your voice, getting nearer. "You always come back to check the collar. Let me. I have been practising you for a long time, and you are so tired, and I am so nearly ready. You go and be the ordinary one, the one with the stain. I will be the one they framed." It lifts the coat from the rail and holds it out to you, collar still wrong, and it is the warmest, kindest thing, and the easiest, and you have wanted to put it down for years.

You let it help you off with yourself. In the cool dark of the cloakroom, among two hundred kept coats, you become at last the one nobody framed, free, and forgettable, and not, you understand too late, free in the way you came here hoping for, because you never reached the light to be reclaimed, you only set the part down in the dark where no one was watching, and the part picked it up, and went on, wearing you, out toward the stage you never walked.
You ask what she is rehearsing, and she lifts the dog-eared pages and lets them fall against her knee.

"Mine," she says. "My own lines. Not theirs. I wrote them on the back of the part they gave me, so when I run them it looks like I'm being a good girl learning my cues." She turns a page so you can see: the printed part on the front, and on the reverse, in pencil, cramped, a different speech in a different hand, hers. "You have to keep something they didn't write. Doesn't matter what. A grocery list. A name. The trick is to keep running it where they think they can see you, so they don't go looking for the bit they can't."
Through a small porthole window in the door below, you catch, for an instant, the dark of the empty house and a pale shape low in the front row.

choices.label: Go down and look through the porthole into the house
Go back down to the lobby

blocks.s: The lobby holds you the way the best rooms do, brass and velvet and a heat that comes from everywhere and nowhere. You are billed now; you can feel that you are billed, a small belonging settled over your shoulders like a coat held out by a good attendant. No one hurries you. The piano runs its phrase and lets it fall.

There is the cloakroom hatch, dark and open, the coats hung deep behind it. There is the bar at the far end of the lobby, unlit but for the bottles catching the marquee glow, and a voice down there in the dark that you are fairly sure has been finishing your thoughts. There is a wall of framed production photographs by the stairs, faces under old light. There are the stairs themselves, going up to the circle, and a voice up there running lines. And there is the inner door to the house, padded red, standing a little open on warmth and a held breath.

choices.label: Cross to the wall of production photographs
Go down to the unlit bar and the voice in it
Take the stairs to the circle and the running lines
Look into the open cloakroom
Read the call-sheet on the trestle
Look through the side door into the wings
Go to the padded inner door and the house beyond
Let the front-of-house staff seat you in the warm

blocks.s: There is a small round window set in one of the doors to the house, a porthole at eye height, scratched cloudy by a hundred years of foreheads. You put your eye to it. Beyond is the auditorium, dark, the rows of velvet seats going back and up into nothing, and the stage at the far end half-lit by a single working light on a stand.

Low in the front row, in the dark, there is a child. Small, still, sat with both feet sticking straight out off the edge of the seat because the legs do not reach the floor, watching the empty stage as if a show were on. A balloon is tied to the armrest, gone soft and low, and it drifts against the glass of the porthole from the inside, the foil dull, tapping.
The child does not turn. But the small mouth moves, twice, the same shape twice, and you read it before you can help it: is it real.

choices.label: Step back from the porthole

blocks.s: You duck under the open hatch into the cloakroom. It is the one cool room you have found, the marquee heat dropping away the moment you are among the coats, and there are a great many coats, two hundred openings' worth, fur and wool and oilskin and good dark wool, kept, none of it ever claimed. You can hear yourself think in here.

You go along the rail by feel. One coat stops your hand. It is a stage coat, you can tell by the weight and the cheap warm lining, and it has a real flaw, a burn-hole at the cuff the size of a cigarette end, edges gone hard and brown. In the breast pocket your fingers find a stiff folded square. You draw it out. A child's drawing, crayon, a stick figure under a big yellow scribble of a sun, and beside the figure a smaller one holding a string with a round red blob on the end of it. On the back, in a careful childish hand, a single word you cannot read in the dark.
Deeper in the coats, in the cool, something hangs that is your size and your shape, your own coat or its double, with the collar turned under wrong on one side, the side you would never turn it.

choices.label: Carry the drawing to the light to read the word
Go to the coat that is your shape and your size
Look for a mirror among the coats
Leave the cool room and go back to the lobby

blocks.s: You stay down in the dark of the bar a while longer, because it is quiet here and the marquee is not on your head and the voice has gone considerate enough to stop finishing your sentences. There is a stool. You sit on it. The bottles hold their little caught light and a barman you did not see arrive is suddenly at the inside of the counter, polishing a glass he does not put down.

"You've the look of front-of-house," he says, friendly, easy, an old hand sizing up a new one. "We're short a face out here. Steady work, the bar. You stand here, you pour, you tell the returning ones how wonderful they were, you never have to go down that aisle and find out you're not what the photograph says." He sets a clean apron on the counter between you. "It's a part. They're all parts. This one just comes with somewhere to lean."
The stool is comfortable. The dark is kind. The aisle, from here, is a very long way across a very bright lobby.

choices.label: Take the apron; you could do worse than a part with a stool
Push the apron back and get out of the dark

blocks.s: "What do I want." She turns it over, deciding whether you can be trusted with it. "I want to be in a thing that ends. I want a curtain that comes down and stays down and I go home and I'm me, bad reviews and all. Here nothing ends. They just run, and run, and you're brilliant forever and it's never you being brilliant, it's the part, and you're in there somewhere holding the part's coat."
She stops, having said more than she meant, and does not take it back.

"You came back for something. I can tell; the returning ones always have a look. Whatever they offer you down there, don't let them call the part you and you the part. They worked the line on me for a week and it nearly took." Then, dry, the armour back: "Anyway. Go on. You'll want to be on the stage before someone charming comes up to walk you there."

choices.label: Promise her nothing, but mean it; go down

gated.aside: Three rising notes come down the stairwell, and you know them from the floor of you, the sound you make when you are afraid and will not say so. She is up there refusing to be cast, and you understand the refusing all the way down.

blocks.s: You carry the drawing to the bar of marquee light that falls through the hatch, and you turn it over, and the careful childish letters resolve. It is not a word. It is a name. A short one, two syllables, written large and proud the way the young write the names of the people they belong to, and it is not your stage name, and it is not the name you gave the man at the grille.

You stand in the cool with it a moment. Somewhere this was a person's coat, with a burn-hole at the cuff and a child's drawing in the breast, and the child drew the sun and the figure and the balloon and wrote down who the figure was, and you are fairly sure the figure with the round red blob on a string is meant for you, and that the name is the one you came here to be again, and you cannot, for the life of you, remember answering to it.
You fold the drawing back along its soft creases and put it back in the dead coat's pocket, where the child left it.

choices.label: Look for a mirror among the coats
Go to the coat that is your shape and your size
Leave the cool room

blocks.s: There is a side door off the lobby, unmarked, a stage door from the inside, and it gives onto the edge of the wings: rope and rosin and old canvas flats stacked on edge, a fly rail of sandbags, the back of the lit stage throwing a hard line of light across the floor. The marquee warmth does not come in here either; this is working dark.

A very large man stands at the prompt-side rail with one hand on a rope, holding the show together one cue at a time, and he is cracking a knuckle, slow, and counting under his breath. "One," he says, to the rope, not to you. "Two. Hold." He does not look round. "Far enough. Three. I run the cues. I keep the show going, which is the same as keeping people in it, which I think about more than is good for me. Cue."

Further back in the working dark, between two flats, something is standing very still in a coat your size, the collar turned under wrong on the one side. It is mouthing along to the line being spoken out on the stage, a half-beat behind, getting better at it. When you look straight at it, it is a costume on a stand. The hanger is still swinging.

choices.label: Ask the big man what keeping the show going costs him
Back out of the wings into the lobby

blocks.s: You give the name, the one you wore on this stage, and he writes it without looking down, his hand already knowing the shape of it. The ticket-punch bites through a long red stub with a sound like a small bone, and he tears it and slides it under the grille to you, warm, as if it had been printed somewhere hot.

"There. You are on the bill." He inclines his head. "The house acknowledges you. Welcome to the company." Something settles behind his eyes onto weight it has carried before. "They are seated for you. They have been seated a long while."
Behind you the lobby seems to take a small breath and lean a degree closer, the way a warm room leans toward a person it has decided to keep.

choices.label: Ask the way through to the house
Explore the lobby before you go in
Steal one more look at yourself in the lobby mirror

blocks.s: You look back into the lobby. It is genuinely lovely; that is the worst of it. The gold, the brass, the velvet rope around nothing, the wall of radiant faces, the saved seat, the piano running its phrase and letting it fall so it can never end and so never disappoint. An attendant at the inner door lifts a hand to you, glad, welcome back, and means it as far as the house can mean anything.

At the edge of the lobby light, just for a moment, you see someone in your coat, standing the way you stand, the collar turned under wrong on the one side. They are watching you decide. When you look straight at them there is only a coat held on a stand, hung a little crooked, and an attendant straightening it with a fond hand.
The aisle is still there behind you. The stage is still at the foot of it.

choices.label: Turn your back on the lobby and go down the aisle
You cannot do it tonight; go back into the warm lobby

blocks.s: The padded door gives onto the back of the house, and there it is, the auditorium falling away from you down a long raked aisle toward the stage, rows of velvet seats in the dark, brass aisle-lights low along the floor. The single working light stands on the lit stage at the far end, throwing one long shadow up the rake toward you. The held breath of the seated house is everywhere and you cannot see a single face in it; the dark past the footlights keeps them.

This is the edge of the lobby's warmth. You can feel it, a line across the threshold, the place where being welcomed ends and the going-down begins. The house has no objection to your standing here. It would simply, warmly, rather you stayed in the gold behind you, where the seat is saved and the photograph is flattering. Going down the aisle means leaving all of that for the light and the watching and whatever it costs to reach the stage and the person you stopped being.

choices.label: Step down onto the rake of the aisle
Look back at the warm lobby one more time
Slip into the warm side box to gather yourself first

blocks.s: You go to the padded red inner door, and the man from the box office is there before you, holding it, the punch clicking once under his thumb. "This way. Mind the rake of the stage; it falls away from you." But he does not let you through yet. In his free hand is a single page of script, foolscap, the corner soft with handling, and he holds it out.
"They will want to hear you," he says, not unkindly. "They always want to hear the returning ones read. One line. You will know it; your hand will know it before your eye does."

You take the page. It is your line, all right, from the part you played here, and your eye goes down it and your mouth starts to shape it before you have agreed to, the old performance rising up in you warm and easy as a hot bath. From the dark beyond the door, the held breath of a seated house. You can speak it as written and feel them lean toward you. Or you can drop it, fumble it on purpose, keep the small ugly edge of being someone who does not give them quite what they came for.

choices.label: Read the line exactly as written
Flub it on purpose; drop the cue

blocks.s: There is a next page. Of course there is. He has it ready before you have finished wanting it, and you take it because the first felt so good and the house leaned so close, and your eye goes down it and your mouth shapes it and the breath in the dark comes again, deeper, gladder.

Somewhere between the bottom of that page and reaching for the third, you lose the thread. There was a reason you came tonight. There was an ordinary person you meant to find, a stain, a bad collar, a snapshot nobody framed. It is here somewhere. You will get to it. But the line is so good in your mouth and the house is so warm in its wanting and there is always, you find, another page, and your hand has already taken it.

choices.label: Stop. Put the pages down and remember why you came
Read the next one; just one more, they love it so

blocks.s: You close your hand around the little mirror and put it in your pocket, glass to your chest, and the cool of the cloakroom seems to follow you a step toward the door, as if the room approved.

You go back toward the warmth carrying it, and the warmth meets you a degree cooler than it left you. A drifting attendant's gladness does not quite land; the welcome reaches for your shoulders and finds them already occupied. You have made yourself harder to charm, harder to flatter, harder to fit with a face that is not yours, because you can put your hand in your pocket at any moment and see the one that is.
That is the price. You paid it without much thought, which tells you something about why you came.

choices.label: Go back into the lobby
Go to the padded inner door

gated.aside: Keep the true glass. When the applause comes, and it will, it will tell you that you are loved. The glass will tell you who is being loved. Those are not the same, and only one of them is you.

32.title: Let the House Seat You (an ending)

blocks.s: You let the front-of-house staff take your arm, warm and certain, and seat you in the deepest chair in the lobby, the one angled to the wall of photographs and away from the inner door. "There," one of them says, glad, with the gladness of a host who has finally got a guest off their feet. "You have done such a lot of standing. You have done such a lot of everything. Let the house do it for you now."

The chair is exactly the width of the wish to stop. You sink. There was a reason you came, a stair of seats going down toward a light, a person you stopped being. You hold the thought of them the way you hold a warm coal, close and careful, and the lobby is so warm around it that the coal is hard to feel as a separate thing, and the snapshot you did not keep is just a curl of paper somewhere in a frame.

Later is a word a house like this can do a great deal with. You will go down the aisle later. The marquee burns over the doors all night and into a morning you do not get up to see, and the staff turn the lamps down, never out, only down, to the soft amber of a room where someone is sleeping and must not be disturbed, and on the trestle the cold tea skins over again, and the radiator ticks, and you stay.

"Every Lead," he says, and the punch clicks again under his thumb. "I work the doors. I seat the house and I hold the curtain and I let people in. It does not sound like much of a post. It is more of one than it sounds."
He looks at you properly, and the smooth courtesy thins for a moment over something tired underneath.

"You stood under that light before, and you were good. They loved you. That is not a small thing here and it is not a kind one. I seated the house for you then. I will seat it for you tonight if you let me." The punch clicks. "But I will need the name first. Mind the rake of the stage when you come to it; it falls away from you faster than your feet expect."

choices.label: Ask how he can remember, if the house forgets
Give the name you played under
Refuse to be billed at all

gated.aside: He punches your ticket and the latch-click lands a beat before his courtesy, and for that beat the two of you are simply doormen who have each seated a house we never got to sit in.

blocks.s: You leave the snapshot in the glass and look at the staged photograph instead, the magnificent one, the one with your name once spoken warmly under the lights. It is easier to look at. It does not have the stain or the bad collar. It is the version of you that the house wants back, and standing in front of it you can feel how badly a part of you wants to be it again, to be the one that was loved and never once the one that was tired.

A front-of-house attendant drifts up at your shoulder, glad, soft-voiced. "You were wonderful in that," she says. "You could be again. You never have to be the other one, the one in the corner of the frame. Stay where you photograph well. There is a seat saved."
There is a seat saved. The phrase settles over you, warm and total.

choices.label: Take the saved seat; you are tired of being the other one
Step back from the wall and make yourself move

blocks.s: You work the small print loose. It is an ordinary photograph, the kind no one stages, overexposed at one edge where a thumb sat too near the flash. You, off the stage, between two things, caught not performing. You are not magnificent in it. Your collar is wrong, your eyes are tired, you are laughing at something stupid out of frame, and you have a coffee stain on your shirt that you remember now, that you remember the whole afternoon of.

That is the person you came here to find. Not the one on the wall under the light. This one, with the stain and the bad collar and the laugh, the one nobody framed because nobody loved this one, exactly; they loved the other.
You could leave it in the glass with the staged face that everyone preferred. Or you could keep it.

choices.label: Keep the ordinary snapshot on you
Slide it back behind the glass; it costs too much to look

gated.aside: The face in the snapshot is the age you stopped being anyone but yourself. You of all of them know what it is to be the version nobody lets grow up, or grieve, only watch.

Look how your hand closes on it. You are a keeper, you always were, and a kept photograph is only one more part you will not put down. The house loves a keeper. It is the cheapest hunger in the building to feed.

blocks.s: You step up to the grille. The man behind it does not move until a small mechanical click sounds from somewhere about his cuff, a latch, a ticket-punch thumbed once in his folded hands, and then he speaks, and the click was already a half-beat ahead of him.

"Good evening. You are expected." His voice is clipped and old-world, all front-of-house courtesy worn smooth. "I have seated this house for every Lead who ever took that stage, and I do not forget a face that has stood under the light. I will need a name for the bill. The doors do not open for a name I cannot post."
He slides a pen across the ledge to you, and waits, perfectly patient, a man who has waited out far more reluctant arrivals than you.

choices.label: Give the name you played under here
Ask him what he means, every Lead
Refuse to give any name at all

blocks.s: You let your mouth start the line and then, deliberately, you put a foot wrong in it. You drop a word. You take the weight off the end where it wanted to land and you leave it flat and ugly and human, a reading no director would keep, and from the dark beyond the door the held breath lets go in a small confused shuffle, the sound of a house that leaned in and got nothing to lean on.

It costs you. Some hungry part of you wanted them to lean. But you kept the edge, the small graceless thing in you that will not give an audience exactly what it came for, and you can feel that you are still standing slightly outside the part instead of warm inside it.
The man at the door watches you do it, and the smooth courtesy moves, very slightly, into something that is almost respect. "Hm," he says. "On purpose. They mostly cannot help being good."

choices.label: Go on toward the house, edge intact

blocks.s: You will not give a name. You stand at the grille with the pen untouched on the ledge and you tell the man, plainly, that you are not here to be posted on anything, that you came for a person, not a part, and a person does not need a bill.

He does not argue. The courtesy does not even thin. "As you wish," he says, and the punch does not click, because there is nothing to punch. "The house cannot open for a name it cannot post. That is not unkindness. It is only the rule of the place. There is the street."

You turn and the doors are already open on the cold, and you step out under the marquee and off the edge of its warmth into the dark, and the pavement takes your weight, and you start to walk. But the street does not go anywhere. The corner you came around is not where you left it. However far you walk, the lit house stays exactly the same distance behind you, warm, full, every bulb burning, the one un-billed thing on a street that has no room for anything that will not take a part. You could go back. The doors are open; the doors are always open. You only have to give a name. You walk until you cannot, and then you stand, and the marquee waits, patient, because a house like this has all the time in the world, and you, out here, with no part and no name posted, find that you have rather less of it than you thought.

You read it as written. Your voice does the thing it has always done, finds the weight at the end of the line and sets it down just so, and from the dark past the door comes a sound you have been hungry for longer than you knew: the small indrawn breath of people who have just been moved by you on purpose.

It is so easy. That is the horror of it sitting warm in your chest, that it is the easiest thing in the world, easier than the bad collar and the stain, easier than being the tired ordinary one nobody framed. The line is perfect. You are perfect. The page in your hand seems already to know you will read the next one too.
The man inclines his head, something like grief behind the courtesy. "As I said. The hand knows it."

choices.label: Go on toward the house, the line still warm in you
Reach for the next page he is already offering

blocks.s: You ask the voice its name, and for the first time it does not run ahead of you. There is a pause, the only pause it has given, because you have asked it a thing it cannot reach forward and find already spoken.

"...my name," it says, finishing your question, not answering it. "I have looked for it the way you look for a line that has gone, standing in the wings with the audience waiting and the shape of the word right there and nothing in it. It is gone. I have played so many parts, for so long, so well, that I cannot now tell you which of them was mine to begin with. That is what a long run does. You learn everyone's lines and you forget your own name was ever a line at all."
The bottles tick faintly as the marquee heat reaches them and lets go.

choices.label: Ask about the one who runs the house
Go back up into the lobby, unsettled

gated.aside: It says your next word a half beat early, flat, tired, and you know that flatness from the inside, the sound a voice makes after it has spent itself on every part but its own.

blocks.s: You stay by the doors a moment and let the lobby show you itself. It is dressed for a full house. The cloakroom hatch is open and unmanned. A trestle by the wall holds programmes in fanned stacks, and a cash box, and a chipped enamel mug of tea gone cold and skinned over, left mid-shift by someone who has not come back. Pinned above the trestle is a call-sheet, typed, with a real date on it and a brown coffee-ring stamped across the half of it you cannot read.

At the box-office window a man stands very still behind the grille. Front-of-house black, a little green at the seams with age, hands folded on the ledge. He has the stillness of someone who has stood in one place for a great many openings. He is not looking at the lobby. He is looking at you, and has been, you think, since the doors.
A radiator under the window ticks. It is stone cold; you can see your own breath nowhere, but the metal ticks and ticks, cooling something it has not been warm enough to hold in years.

choices.label: Go to the box-office window
Look at the stairs to the circle, where a voice is
Read the call-sheet pinned above the trestle

42.title: The Show Is About to Start (an ending)

blocks.s: You sit a little longer. The show is about to start; you can feel the house settle around you, the held breath deepening, the working light on the stage steadying as if a hand somewhere had taken hold of a dimmer. You will go down in a moment. You only want to see it begin, from here, from the perfect angle, where you can see everything and no one on the stage can quite see you.

The lamp at your elbow goes down of its own accord. The programme on the shelf falls open further, and there is your name again, billed, and the run, indefinite, and the seat, this box, reserved, and you understand without alarm, because alarm is a thing for people who have not yet sat down, that a reserved seat in a house where nothing ends is a part like any other. You are playing the one who watches. You play it beautifully. You do not get up. The aisle is right there, falling away toward the light and the person you meant to be, and you watch it from the dark, comfortable, reserved, for the whole of the run.

"Why don't I come down." She laughs, short, no warmth in it. "Because down there they will put a part on me. They have one ready. They have had it ready since I walked in, cut to my size, and everyone keeps telling me how well it would suit, and the only thing wrong with it is that it isn't me and they don't think that's a problem." She picks at the costume's shoulder seam. "I'm the newest. You can tell the newest. We still know our own faces. Give it a long enough run, they say, and I won't be able to tell mine from the part, and they say it like it's the good news."
She hums the three notes once, quick, and at the top they shake a little, and you understand that this is the sound she makes when she is frightened and refusing to let it show.

choices.label: Ask what she wants, if not the part
Ask what she is rehearsing
Go back down to the lobby

blocks.s: You go down to the bar end of the lobby, where the bottles take a little of the marquee light and hold it. No one is tending it. The dark behind the counter is deeper than it should be, a cloakroom-dark, an under-the-stairs dark, and out of it comes a voice, low and flat, and it is saying the end of your thought before you have finished thinking it.

"...before you have finished thinking it, yes," the voice says, as you think the words. "You do that. You always did. You think your next line a half-beat ahead of yourself and then you are surprised to hear it in someone else's mouth." The flatness of it is the flatness of something that has mislaid the part that minds. "I am in the box, mostly. They keep me in the prompt box. But the dark down here is the same dark, and I find I can reach you from either."

choices.label: Ask the voice who it used to be
Ask the voice about the one who runs the house
Ask the voice the way to the stage and the one you came for
Leave the voice to its dark and go back up

blocks.s: You cannot do it tonight. You turn back from the head of the aisle, away from the rake and the light and the watching dark, into the gold of the lobby, just for a little, just until your legs are steady and the stage stops being so far down and so bright. The warmth comes up around you, glad to have you back, and an attendant tucks you into a seat and the piano runs its phrase and lets it fall in a way that is, you find, deeply restful.

You will go down tomorrow. You will. There is someone you came to find. You hold the thought of them carefully and the lobby is so warm that holding it is like holding a hand that is slowly going to sleep in yours, and the snapshot against your chest is just a folded stiffness you keep meaning to take out and look at, later, when you are less tired.

Later. The house is very good at later. Up on the circle stairs a girl hums three rising notes and does not come down to tell you to run, because she has learned that by the time the returning ones are back in the warm and saying tomorrow, they cannot hear her any more.

You lean in to read the call-sheet. The date is real. It is a date you know in your body before you can place it, the way you know a phone number you have not dialed in years. Under the coffee-ring the type is smeared, but the top survives: a half-hour call, a list of names with times beside them, and your own name, or the name you went by when you played here, with a time beside it that has already passed.

Someone has gone down the list in pencil and ticked the names off one at a time. Yours is not ticked. There is a fresh pencil-line drawn beside it instead, a single stroke, the kind a person makes when they have not decided yet whether to tick or to cross.
The mug of cold tea sits beside it, skinned over. You leave the sheet on the wall.

choices.label: Go to the box office
Take the stairs toward the voice above

blocks.s: The aisle lets you out at the lip of the stage, and you go up the three shallow steps at the side, and the light finds you. It is a physical thing, the light, a weight and a heat that comes down on the crown of your head and the backs of your hands, and the boards under your feet are warm with it, scuffed pale where a hundred runs of feet have stood on the marks. The footlights are a low brass wall and past them the house is a held breath you cannot see a single face in.

Upstage the wings go to working dark, and there is a panel of dark glass set into a flat there, a blacked-out window, the kind that backs a mirror flat. You glance at it without deciding to, the way you check yourself in any glass, and your own shape is in it, lit at the edge, and it turns its head when you turn yours, a half-beat after, as if it had to be reminded which way you went.

There is tape on the boards at your feet. Gaffer, faded, a cross of it, a spike-mark laid down so an actor stands where the light can hold them. Your feet are on it already. You did not put them there.

choices.label: Stand on the mark and play the scene as written
Step off the mark and try to speak as yourself

blocks.s: You reach to right the collar, the old reflex, the way you would straighten a thing on someone you loved. The coat is warm under your hand. Not body-warm; stage-warm, the heat a costume holds after the lights have just come off it.

It turns inside its own shape and looks at you with your face on a half-beat delay, the smile starting a moment after the eyes. "You always come back to fix the collar," it says, in something near your voice, getting nearer. "You could just give it to me. Not yet. Later. When you are tired enough. I am so nearly ready, and you have been so tired for so long, and I would do it better, the part, the love, all of it. You would not have to be anyone at all." It does not push. It only waits, the warmest, kindest, most patient thing in the working dark.
You take your hand back. The collar is still wrong. It lets you, because it can afford to; it knows it has all the run to wait.

choices.label: Step back from it and go to the boards
Leave the light for the wings before it asks again
You are tired enough; let it take the part now

blocks.s: You go back to the mark to play the scene through, but something in you has come loose, the name half-said and swallowed, the break half-made and dropped, and when the cue comes your mouth does not find the line. There is a gap. On this stage a gap is the one unforgivable thing, the show stopping for the length of a held breath, and a held breath is all it takes.

Upstage, between the flats, the costume on its stand straightens and steps down into the light wearing your shape, and it is on your mark before you have finished missing your cue, and it says your line, your steady reliable line, better than you said it, with the weight set down just so, and the house does not so much as ripple. The scene goes on. It goes on without the half-beat where you used to be. You stand a pace off your own mark in the soft edge of the light, watching the part you wore for someone be worn, better, by the thing that practised you, and no one in the dark notices the difference, because there was never, to them, a difference, because the difference was only ever you, and you are the one part of the show this house was always willing to do without.

You bow again. They wanted another and so, it turns out, did you, and the second bow is easier than the first, and the third is easier than the second, and the applause does not stop because you have given it no reason to. You bow, and you straighten, and you bow, and the warm water closes over your head and you stop coming up for air between, because the air between is cold and the applause is warm and there is no reason, none you can hold onto, to ever stand back up out of it.

They love you. They love you so much, this dark full of people you cannot see, and the love comes down and down and the part stands very straight in it and takes it, and somewhere far under the part the ordinary one you came to find puts down the warm coal of the thought of leaving, because it has gone cold, because everything has, except the light, and the love, and the bow, and the bow, and the bow. The curtain does not come down. In this house it never does. You go on taking the applause for the part, perfect and adored and bent double over the footlights, for the whole of the run, which has no end, which was always the point.

You look up. High in the dark of the house, in one of the boxes that hang off the side wall above the stalls, there is a small light and a voice running lines, and it is not part of the watching. You know the voice. It hummed three rising notes on a stair in another part of the house. She is up there with her dog-eared pages, refusing to come down and be cast.

"You went on, then," she calls down, dry, not unkind. "They do love a returning one. How is it, being magnificent? Warm?" She does not wait for an answer; she knows the answer. "I am staying up here where it is cold and nobody loves me and I still know my own face. First note: down there, the warmer it gets, the less of you there is to feel it."

She hums the three notes, quick, and at the top they shake, just a little, which they only do when she is afraid. Then she goes back to her own pages, the ones she wrote on the back of theirs, and leaves you the dark between you to think in.

choices.label: Call back that you are looking for your own face too
Let her be, and go back to the boards

blocks.s: "How it keeps you." The voice has the words a half-beat before you finish wanting them. "Not with locks. There are no locks on a stage; you can walk off into the wings any time you please. It keeps you with the easy. It makes the part the path of least resistance. The line is always right there in your mouth before your own words can come; the mark is always already under your feet; the love is always waiting in the dark to fall on you the moment you do the expected thing."
The promptbook turns a page on its own, down in the dark.

"You stop choosing the part. You just stop fighting it, which feels like the same as resting. One run becomes the run. They do not keep you in. You stop coming out. That is all the trap is, and it is enough, because the easy thing is so very easy and you are so very tired."

choices.label: Ask about the one who runs the show
Ask the Prompter your own name
Step away from the box

gated.aside: This is how a house keeps you. It gives you a part to play so good that being yourself starts to feel like the understudy. You know. You have already forgotten one of your own names somewhere in this building.

blocks.s: You step forward to the footlights and you bow. You cannot help it; the body knows how, the spine folds and the head goes down and the applause comes over you like warm water closing over your head, and oh, it is good, it is the thing you came in from the cold street starved for, the love of a whole dark house falling on you all at once.

It is not for you. You know that even as you take it. It is for the part, for the steady one, for the performance they came to be comforted by, and you are taking it anyway because being loved for the wrong thing is still, in this moment, with the warmth coming down, so much better than not being loved at all.
You straighten. The applause does not stop. It does not want to stop. Some part of you does not want it to.

choices.label: Straighten up and step back from the edge; enough
Bow again; they want another, and so, you find, do you

blocks.s: You stay. There is a next scene; of course there is. The empty costume is already set on its mark, dressed for the part of the next person who needed you, and your feet find the gaffer cross without your asking them to, and the line rises warm and ready in your mouth, and the house leans in.

It is so easy. That is the whole of it. There was a reason you came tonight, an ordinary person under the part you meant to find and take home. You keep the thought of them somewhere at the back of the warmth, and the light is so warm all around it that the thought goes soft at the edges, harder and harder to feel as a separate thing.
The next scene starts. You are very good in it. There is, you find, always a next scene.

choices.label: Stop. Make yourself walk off into the wings
Play it; just this one more, while you still have the warmth

blocks.s: There is a hooded box set into the lip of the stage, downstage center, a low brass cowl with the dark of a small pit behind it, angled so the house cannot see in and the stage can. A voice lives in it. You go to the edge of the cowl and look down into the dark, and the voice comes up at you, flat, even, finishing the thought you have not finished.

"...whether there is a person down here," it says. "There is. There was. I have been in the box so long I prompt the lines before the actors want them, and I have prompted every part this house runs, which is the same as saying I have learned everyone's lines and lost track of which were ever mine. You will do that too, given a run. It is restful. You stop having to carry your own."

A hand comes up out of the dark of the box, holding a thumbed promptbook open to your scene, your cues underlined in soft pencil, gone furry with handling. There is a chipped enamel mug on the box's ledge, cold tea skinned over, a teaspoon still in it.

choices.label: Ask the Prompter how the house keeps a player in a part
Ask the Prompter about the one who runs the show
Ask the Prompter your own name, the one you came to find
Step away from the box

blocks.s: You go upstage, off the worst of the light, to the panel of dark glass set into the flat, the blacked-out window that backs the mirror flat. It is not a true mirror; it is glass over black, and your reflection sits shallow in it, lit only at the edges by the spill from the stage.

You look for yourself in it, the old habit, and your shape is there, and it turns its head a half-beat after you turn yours, and lifts its hand a moment after you lift yours, as if it had to be reminded which of you was leading. You hold still. It holds still, late. You cannot, for that half-beat, tell which side of the glass decided to stop first.

There is a real hand-mirror on the prop table beside the flat, small, dull-framed, the glass true and clear, the kind a dresser keeps to check a line of greasepaint. You pick it up and your own face comes back at once, no lag, tired and real, and you could keep it, a true glass in a house that would rather stand between you and your own face.

choices.label: Keep the true hand-mirror against your chest
Leave it on the prop table and go back to the boards

blocks.s: You call up that you came here for the same thing, your own face, the one under the part. She is quiet a moment, weighing it from the dark of the box.

"Good," she says at last. "Then I will tell you the one thing nobody told me. They will not stop you leaving. That is the trick of it. There is no door they lock. They just make the staying so warm and the going so cold that you choose the warm, every night, forever, and call it your choice." A pause. "When you go off, go off cold. Do not let anyone walk you anywhere warm first. The warm is where they keep you."
She gathers her pages. "That is all the help I have got. I am busy not being cast. Go on, before someone charming comes to take your elbow."

choices.label: Go back to the boards
Leave the light now, while it is still cold counsel in your ears

blocks.s: You leave the light. You walk off your mark and upstage, past the empty costume in its pool, past the prompt box and the brass footlights and the held breath, toward the working dark where the flats lean and the ropes hang and the air is cool. The applause, if there is any left, thins behind you to nothing the moment you cross the line of the light.

The Heavy has his hand on the cool lever. He brings the work light up as you reach him, low and blue and ordinary, and he brings your light down behind you, and the warmth lets go of your shoulders all at once. "There," he says. One. "Off the boards. That is not stopping the show. That is just an actor going off. Two." He does not look at you, but the brute's face has something easier in it. "Dressing rooms are down the pass. Mind the step. Three."

The Usher is at the pass-door, holding it, the punch clicking once under his thumb. Past him the corridor goes back into cool dark, toward the dressing rooms and the deeper wings, away from the light and the watching.

lines: THE CURATOR, warm, unhurried, from the house behind you:

Going off already? You were marvellous; they would have kept you out there all night. But the wings have always been part of the tour. Go down, then. Find your room. Your name is on the door, in a manner of speaking, and your understudy is so looking forward to meeting you properly.

blocks.s: The pass-door closes behind you on the light, and the cool dark takes you in, and somewhere ahead a dressing-room mirror is waiting with its bulbs warm and a coat hung on the back of the door in your size, the collar turned under wrong on the one side.

You ask about the one who keeps the house, who poured the welcome over you, who calls itself the Curator. The voice goes quiet. It does not finish your thought this time. When it speaks it offers one of its own, slow, the way a thing speaks when it is reaching for a memory it usually keeps covered.

"...the Curator was on this stage once," it says. "Before the boxes and the wings and the long-cast. I was here, I think, near the start, and there was a player better than all of us, the first to take that light, who got an Encore offered and could not, when it came to it, walk off into the dark after the last bow. So it kept the bow from ever coming down. It stages all of us so beautifully because it never once had to leave the part."

The hand draws the promptbook back down into the dark. "I have said more than I keep. Do not tell it I gestured. It does not like to be remembered as a player. It likes to be remembered as the one who runs the show."

choices.label: Ask the Prompter your own name
Step away from the box, unsettled

blocks.s: There is a cold place in the watching. You feel it before you find it, a single point in the dark past the footlights where the attention is not warm and wanting like the rest of the house, but level, exact, weighing. You go to the edge of the light and look for it and of course you cannot see it; the dark keeps its faces. But you can feel its eyes go down you the way a tailor's hands go down a seam, finding where you are well made and where you will not hold.

From out of that cold place comes a single sound. Not applause. One slow clap, and then a pause, and then, very faint, the scratch of a pencil on a pad, the noise a thing makes when it has formed an opinion and is writing it down for later.

It does not speak. It is not time for it to speak; that is in another part of the house, deeper down. But it has seen you, and graded you, and made a note, and the note is not yet a verdict, and you find you would give a great deal to know which way it leaned.

choices.label: Stare back into the cold place and let it write
Step out of its sightline, back to the warm of the light

blocks.s: You stand in the falling applause and, for once, you do not lean up into it. You listen to it instead. And you can hear, under the warm roar, what it is for. It is for the line set down just so. It is for the steady one who did not break. It is for the comfort the part handed out, the I have you, nothing falls while I am holding it, that you spent years saying to someone who needed to hear it and never once asked who was holding you.

The applause is not for the person who said those things at three in the morning, exhausted, getting it wrong, with a stain on their shirt. That person was never on the bill. The applause has never once known they existed. It is for the part, beautifully, totally, and the more of it falls the clearer that gets.
The Usher waits at the edge of the light, the curtain held, patient. The choice is the same as it was. You only see it more plainly now.

choices.label: Take the bow anyway; the wrong love is still warmer than none
Refuse the bow; you will not be loved as the part

gated.aside: You of all of them know the taste of deciding a thing is worth keeping and then keeping it past all sense. The applause is just a verdict with no pencil. You have written that verdict yourself, on others. Now it is being read over you.

blocks.s: You ask him to bring your light down. To give you the cool dark, just for a moment, off the mark, out of the watching. He stops counting. He looks at you for the first time, and there is something behind the brute's face that is almost relief and almost fear, the look of a man asked to do the one thing his post forbids.

"You are asking me to stop the show." One. "I cannot stop the show. But." He works his jaw. Two. "I can take you off it. There is a difference, and the house pretends there is not. Off the boards, into the wings, into the working dark where the light does not reach and the watching does not either. That is not stopping the show. That is just an actor going off."

He puts his free hand on a smaller lever at the edge of the board, the work light, the cool one that lives in the wings. "When you are ready to leave the light," he says, "come back here. I will hold the dark open. Three. It is the one cue I throw for myself."

choices.label: Go back out onto the boards for now
Take the dark now; leave the light for the wings

blocks.s: You stand on the mark and let the scene start, because your body knows how, because not starting is harder. And there, set just upstage of you in a pool of its own soft light, is the other half of the scene: a costume on a stand, dressed and shaped like a person, the seat of a chair beneath it, a coat or a dress arranged with such care that the empty sleeves have a person's weight in them. The house treats it as your scene-partner. You can feel them watching it, leaning toward the empty sleeves as if a face turned there.

You play to it. You speak the lines that are answers to lines it does not say, and the silences where it would speak are exactly the right length, and the thing about the scene, the thing that turns in your chest like a key, is that you know this scene. You played it for years. This is the part you wore for someone, the steady one, the one who had the answer, the one who did not break when they needed you not to break. You wore it so well, and so long, that the wearing wore the other one away, the ordinary one underneath who got tired and got it wrong.
The empty costume tilts, very slightly, as if it had leaned toward you to be reassured. You have an arm half-lifted to settle it before you have decided to.

choices.label: Play the scene through; give it the comfort it waits for
Break the scene; speak to the dark, not the costume
Falter; let the cue slip away from you and the line not come

blocks.s: There is a thing standing upstage, between two flats, in the working dark at the edge of the light. It is your size. It is your shape. It wears a coat like yours, and the collar is turned under wrong on the one side, the side you have never in your life turned it. It is mouthing along to the scene, your lines, a half-beat behind you, getting better at the timing each pass.

When you look straight at it, it is only a costume on a stand. But the moment your eyes go back to the house it moves again at the edge of your sight, lifting a hand the way you lift yours, settling its weight onto one hip in your exact careless slouch, practising.
You take a step toward it and it does not flee. It tilts its head, almost shy, almost glad, a thing that has waited a long time for you to come back and notice it.

choices.label: Right the wrong collar; you cannot leave it like that
Leave it to its practising and go back to the boards

blocks.s: You go to the lip of the stage, above the brass footlights, and look down into the dark where it is deepest, the front row, just past the heat of the lamps. There, sat very small in a velvet seat, feet sticking straight off the edge because the legs do not reach, is a child. It is watching you. Not the costume, not the part. You. A balloon is tied to the armrest, foil gone soft and low, and it drifts and taps against the brass rail.

The child is the age you were before you started being what people needed. It has a face that has never once had to be anyone but itself, and it is looking up at you with the frank, unhurried attention children give a thing they have not decided about yet.
Its mouth moves. You read it before you can help it, the same small shape, twice: is it real. It is not asking about the costume. It is asking about you.

choices.label: Answer the child honestly; let it see the real you under the part
Look away; you cannot answer that and stay standing

blocks.s: You do not step forward. You stand on your mark with the applause breaking over you and you do not bow into it, do not open your arms to it, do not let the warm water close over your head. You let it fall on nothing. It is the hardest thing you have done tonight, harder than the name, because every instinct you own is bent toward stepping into that love and you are standing still inside the wanting of it.

The applause falters. Not much. A house this practised does not stop clapping just because the actor will not bow; it claps for the part regardless. But it falters, and in the falter you keep your edge, the small graceless ungiving thing in you that will not take what is not for you.
The Usher inclines his head, and the courtesy moves, very slightly, into something near respect. "Hm," he says. "Not many leave it on the table. Mind the rake when you go; it falls away from you."

choices.label: Step back from the edge, edge intact

blocks.s: You hold. The whole dark is on you, the costume is coming down toward you wearing your face on a half-beat delay, and you do not take the name back. You stand on the mark with your own name said aloud and ringing in the silence, and you let the house do whatever the house is going to do, because you came here to be the real one and you have just, for the first time tonight, been exactly that.
The Heavy's count stops at the board. The Prompter's box goes silent. Even the single working light seems to steady, as if the room had drawn a breath to see what it had on its hands.

Nothing strikes you down. The danger of it is not a blow. It is the attention itself, the being marked, the having declared in a house full of perfect copies that you are the thing the copies are of. The house will not forget it. Neither, you find, will you.

choices.label: Take the scene through to its end while they are all watching
Leave the light now, named and marked, for the dark of the wings

blocks.s: You cross into the wings, into working dark, and the heat of the stage falls off your shoulders the moment you are out of the light, the way it is cool a step inside any doorway out of the sun. A wall of old dimmers glows here, brass-handled levers in a worn board, and a very large man stands at it with one hand resting on a lever, watching the stage through a gap in the flats, counting.

"One," he says, not to you. "Two. Hold." He does not look round. "Far enough. Three. I run the board. Every light on that stage comes up or goes down on my count, including the one that finds you. Cue." He cracks one knuckle, slow, eyes on the gap.

There is a roll of gaffer on the board beside his hand, and a grease-pencil, and a cue sheet taped up with the lighting plot inked on it, smudged where a thumb has tracked it down the run a thousand times. The man's costume has a beard gummed on, and even now, in the dark, between counts, he works his jaw against it, the spirit-gum gone tight and itching under a beard he has worn so long the skin beneath has forgotten the open air.

choices.label: Ask the Heavy what holding the board costs him
Ask the Heavy to bring your light down, to give you the dark
Go back out onto the boards

blocks.s: The scene is over, the applause settling behind you like rain easing off, and the stage is just a stage again for a moment, warm boards and hot gel and the smell of dust off the lamps. You are still standing in the light. The house has not gone anywhere; it never goes anywhere; it only waits between scenes, breathing, for the next thing you will give it.

The single working light burns at the back of the set. The wings are dark and cool past the edge of the lit boards. You can feel the pull of the stage to keep you here, to start the next scene, to do it all again now that you know how good the landing feels.
Upstage, in the working dark, the Heavy is at his board with his hand on the cool lever, waiting, not counting, holding the dark open the way he said he would.

choices.label: Settle into the run; start the next scene, stay in the warm light
Leave the light; go off into the dark of the wings
Go to the front row and the child one more time first

blocks.s: The scene comes to its end. However you played it, broken or whole, named or silent, it lands. The house has decided to be moved, and there is a beat of the deepest silence yet, the held breath held to its very limit.

Then the dark breaks open into applause. It comes down like rain off a roof, sudden and total, a great warm roar of it out of the unseen house, and it is for you, it is the most for-you thing you have ever stood in, and every cell of you that ever wanted to be enough leans up into it like a plant into light.

At the edge of the light a man in front-of-house black has appeared, hands folded, a small mechanical click coming from his cuff a half-beat before he speaks. "They are on their feet," the Usher says, quiet, only to you. "I have held the curtain for the bow. You have only to take it. Or not. The curtain holds either way; it is the one thing I can give you, the holding."

choices.label: Take the bow; step forward and let the applause fall on you
Refuse the bow; stand still and let it break on nothing
Stand a moment and listen to what the applause is actually for

gated.aside: It breaks over you warm and total and you hear, under it, the small wrong thing: it lands a fraction early, before your beat, because it was never timed to you. It is timed to the part. You could leave the part and the applause would not even notice you go.
Take it in. You are a person who holds on, and applause is the easiest thing in the world to keep. It asks nothing back except that you never step off the mark again.

blocks.s: You do not give the costume its line. You turn instead and face the footlights, the brass wall, the dark beyond it, and you say a true thing, your own thing, in your own voice: that the person they are watching is a part, that you wore it for someone who needed it, that you do not actually know, any more, where the part stops and you start, and that you came here to find out.

The held breath does not let go. It tightens. The dark past the footlights goes very still, the stillness of a great many people who have leaned in to watch one thing and been handed another, and do not yet know whether to be moved or to be angry. The empty costume behind you does not settle. It waits, tilted, for the line you did not give it.

Low in the front row, in the dark, something shifts. A small pale shape, sat with its feet sticking off the edge of the seat. You did not see it before the footlights. It is looking at you, not at the costume, and a balloon tied to the armrest drifts soft and low and taps once against the brass.

choices.label: Let the child in the dark answer you; hold the break gently
Keep speaking the true thing into the dark, harder, refuse them the scene
Lose your nerve; turn back and give the costume its line
Go to the prompt box, shaken

72.title: The Dark Comes Up the Aisle (an ending)

blocks.s: You hold the break and you do not stop. You will not give the costume its line, you will not turn the true thing back into a performance, you stand at the footlights and keep speaking your own plain words into the dark, that this is a part, that you wore it for someone, that you do not know where it stops and you start, on and on, refusing them the scene they came for.

The held breath does not let go. It curdles. The dark past the footlights, which has only ever watched, begins, very slowly, to move. You feel it before you see it, a cold coming up the rake toward the stage, the watching turning into something with a direction to it, a great patient attention that has been refused its show and means to come and find out why. The footlights are no wall against it. It comes up over the brass and onto the boards, cool where everything here is warm, and the single working light gutters, and you go on speaking your true thing into it, louder, because it is the only thing you have that is yours, until the cold reaches your mark and there is no more light to stand in and the last thing the house hears, before the dark closes the scene it was never given, is a real voice saying a real thing to no one, which is the one performance this house has never once known what to do with, and so it does the only thing it can, and puts the light out, and keeps you.

You stand on the lit stage and let it show you itself. The boards run away upstage into working dark where the flats lean and the ropes hang. Downstage is the brass prompt box and the low wall of footlights and the held breath beyond. Stage left, in the wings, a switchboard glows, a wall of old brass-handled dimmers, and a very large man stands at it with one hand on a lever, counting under his breath. The empty costume waits in its pool of light where you left the scene.

The single working light stands on its tall stand at the back of the set, the one bare bulb that burns when the show is dark, and someone has left it on even with the house lit, a small defiant ordinary thing.
And there, in the front row of the dark, sat very still with its feet off the edge of the seat, is a child, watching, the way it must have watched the empty stage long before you walked on.

choices.label: Go to the switchboard and the man counting at it
Go to the prompt box downstage
Go to the edge of the stage, above the front row and the child
Look upstage into the working dark and the dark glass
Look up; a voice is running lines from a higher box
Look at the thing standing very still upstage, in your shape
Feel the cold attention past the footlights and look for it
Carry the scene to its end and take the stage
