blocks.s: You close your hand around the little mirror and put it in your inside pocket, glass to your chest. The cool of the working dark seems to follow you a step, as if the wings approved of a thing the stage would not.

You carry it back toward the light and the warmth meets you a degree cooler than it left you, the welcome reaching for your shoulders and finding them already taken up with something it cannot flatter. You have made yourself harder to fit with a face that is not yours. You can put your hand in your pocket at any moment and find the one that is.
That is the price, and you paid it without much thought, which tells you something about why you came.

choices.label: Go back out onto the boards
Take the scene to its end and the applause

blocks.s: You ask the Prompter for your name, the real one, the one you came back to find under the part. For once the voice does not run ahead of you. There is a pause, the only pause it has given you, the silence of a thing trying to reach forward into a line and finding the page blank.

"...your name," it says at last, not answering, only finishing the question. "I could prompt you any line in this house but that. It is the one cue the book does not hold. A name is not a line; it is the thing under the lines, and this place has no use for the thing under the lines, so it does not write them down." A breath that is not a breath. "You could say it yourself. Out loud, on the boards, into the dark. It would cost you. I would not do it. But you could; you are still original enough to have one to say."
The promptbook does not turn. The cold tea sits skinned over on the ledge.

choices.label: Step away and think on it
Say your true name aloud, here, now, into the dark

gated.aside: It says your next word a half beat early, flat, tired, and you know that flatness from the inside, the sound a voice makes after it has spent itself on every part but its own.

blocks.s: You step off the gaffer cross. It is a small thing, a half-pace, but the light does not come with you cleanly; it holds where the mark is and you stand at the soft edge of it, half in shadow, and the boards there are colder, the way the floor is colder a foot back from a fire.

You open your mouth to say something of your own, your actual voice, a plain sentence about why you came. What comes up instead is the line. It rises in you warm and ready, the old performance, your mouth already shaping it before your own thought can find the words. You shut your teeth on it. From the dark past the footlights there is a small shift, a settling, the sound a room full of people makes when the thing they expected does not quite happen on time.

From somewhere downstage, low, a flat voice arrives a half-beat ahead of your next thought. "...harder than it looks," it says, finishing the thing you were about to think. "Standing off your own mark. They built the light to fit the part, not you. Stand where it wants you and it is easy. Stand here and you have to do all the holding yourself."

choices.label: Find the voice; it came from the prompt box
Go back to the mark and play the scene

blocks.s: The attention is too much. The whole dark turned on you at once, the warmth gone out of it, the copy coming down toward you wearing your face, and the nerve goes out of you like water out of a cracked glass. You take it back. You say, no, no, that was a line, that was in the scene, forget it, I did not mean it, and you reach for the part the way a falling person reaches for any handhold, and you pull it back over yourself, and you give the house the steady line it was waiting for after all.

The warmth comes back. The dark un-turns. The copy stops on the edge of the light and waits, patient, because it can afford to be; it knows now what you are, which is someone who will say the true thing once and then beg to be let back into the lie the moment the room goes quiet. The applause comes, kind, forgiving, a little pitying. You bow. You said your name once, out loud, into the dark, the realest thing you had, and then you handed it back across the footlights and asked them please to love the part instead, and they do, they will, forever, and you spend the rest of the run knowing exactly what you traded and exactly how cheap you let it go.

You go back to the mark, into the full weight of the light, and you do the thing the Prompter would not do. You say your name. Your true one, the one under all the parts, the one the child's drawing carried and you could not remember answering to. You say it plainly, out loud, into the dark past the footlights.

The house changes. The held breath stops being a held breath and becomes attention, total and turned, the whole watching dark swinging round onto you like a single slow head. The warmth goes out of it. A name on this stage is a claim; it says I am the original, I am the one the part was cut from, I am not the performance. And the house has been waiting, you feel now, a very long time, to find out which of the things on its boards was the real one.

Upstage, between two flats, something that was a costume on a stand straightens, and turns, and begins, slowly, to come down toward the light, wearing your shape, the collar turned under wrong on the one side.

choices.label: Hold your ground; you said it, you mean it, you are the real one
The attention is too much; take it back, beg back into the part

blocks.s: You play the next one. After all, the warmth is right there and the line is in your mouth and it would be a small cruelty to the house, leaning in like that, to stop now. The scene lands. The next costume is set on its mark before the applause has finished. You play that one too.

Somewhere among the scenes the reason you came goes quietly out, the way the working light gets switched off at the end of a night when everyone has gone home and there is no one left to see by. There was an ordinary person under the part. There was a name you could not remember answering to. It is here somewhere, behind you, in the dark past the footlights you stopped looking into. The house lights never come up and the curtain never comes down and there is always, always a next scene, and you are so good in all of them, and there is no longer anyone inside the part to mind that the love falling on it like rain was never once for them. The show runs on. You are the run now. It is the most comfortable thing in the world, and that is the whole of what it cost.

You are so tired. That is the whole of it, in the end, not despair and not surrender, only a tiredness that has been in you for years, since long before this house, since the first night you decided someone else's need was bigger than your own face. You tell the thing in your shape, yes. Take it. I am done holding it.

It is so glad. It steps into the light wearing you and rights its own wrong collar with your hands and goes down to the mark and stands in the heat as if it had been made for it, which it was, by you, over all those years. The house does not notice the change. There was never, to them, anything to notice; the part is the part. The applause comes, and it falls on the copy now, warm and total and exactly as adored, and the copy takes it beautifully, and bows, and is loved.

You stand in the working dark at the edge of it, in your own ordinary unwatched body, free of the part at last, and you wait to feel reclaimed, to feel like the person you came to find. But you never reached the light to be reclaimed in it; you only set the part down in the dark where no one was looking, and it picked you up off the floor of yourself and put you on, and now it is out there being loved, wearing you, and you are back here, in the cold, free in every way except the one you came for.

You play it through. You give the empty costume the line it is waiting for, the steady one, the one that says I have you, nothing is going to fall while I am holding it, and your voice does the old reliable thing, finds the bottom of the line and rests its weight there, and the costume settles under the words as if a person inside it had let out a long-held breath.

It is good work. It is the best work you have. From the dark past the footlights the held breath deepens; you are doing the thing they came to see, the thing they loved, and the loving comes off them in a warmth that is almost the heat of the lamps.
A flat voice from the box downstage has your next line a half-beat before you reach it. You do not need it. You never needed it; that is the trouble. The part runs in you like a tap left on.

choices.label: Go to the prompt box and the voice in it
Look out into the dark, where someone small is sitting
Carry the scene to its end and the applause

blocks.s: You crouch at the lip of the stage, close as the footlights let you, and you answer the child the only honest way you have left, which is to stop performing for a moment and let your face do whatever a tired real face does when no one is supposed to be looking. You drop the part. You let it see the one underneath, the ordinary one, the one with the bad collar and the wrong answers, the one nobody framed.

The child considers you. Then it nods, small and certain, the way children settle a question, and the balloon bobs once against the rail, and you understand, in the plainest way, that for one moment something in this house has looked straight at you and seen the real thing and known it for real.

It does not fix anything. The part is still there waiting on its mark. But you have proof now, a witness in the dark, that there is still a you to be seen under the performance, and the house cannot un-know that it was seen.

choices.label: Stand, steadier than before, and go back to the boards
Take the scene to its end and the applause
Leave the light now, while you can still feel yourself

blocks.s: "What it costs." Four. He cracks the knuckle again, jaw working at the itch under the gummed beard. "I keep the show running. That is the job. Keep it running means keep the people in it; a show with an empty mark is a show that stopped, and the one rule here is the show does not stop. Five. So I hold the levers and I throw the cues and when one of you wanders toward the dark in the middle of a run, I am the one whose hand is on the switch that brings your light back up and walks you back to your mark."

He says the next bit to the dimmers, low. "Worst of it is I am good at it. I am big, and the show wants a big thing that does not ask why, and I have got a temper somewhere in me that likes having a wall to stand at instead of a person to stand over. Six."
Then, dry, between counts, the nearest he comes to a joke: "They think the show is the hard part. The lines, the light, the love. Show's the easy part. Coming off after is the part nobody can do."

choices.label: Ask him to bring your light down and give you the dark
Go back out onto the boards

blocks.s: You come off the stage into the backstage warren, and the light changes the moment the wings take you, the hot white of the boards giving way to a low working blue, a single bare bulb on a wire strung down the corridor of dressing rooms. The heat lets go of the back of your neck. The watching lets go. Back here it is cool and unwatched, the safe register, the smell of cold cream and dust and old steam pipes, and you can hear your own heart for the first time since the lobby.

The doors run down one side, numbered, and one of them has a card in a brass slot with a name on it, and the name is yours, the one you wore on this stage, in someone's careful hand. The door is ajar. Inside, the counter glows: a long mirror ringed with bulbs, half of them burned dark, and someone is sitting at it with their back to you, in your coat, and the collar is turned under wrong on the one side, the side you would never turn.

You lift a hand to the doorframe, and out of habit you glance into the strip of mirror you can see past the figure's shoulder, to find your own face. The face that answers is yours, and it lifts its hand to the frame the same as you, and it does it a half-beat late.

choices.label: Step in and speak to the one in your coat
Back out quietly and look for the way out

blocks.s: You sit, because your legs want to, and you ask it what it is.

"A copy," it says, without flinching, dabbing at its own jaw with cold cream on a wad of cotton, taking off a face to show you it can. "Your understudy. They made me out of everything they loved about you, which is to say everything that was the part and nothing that was you. I am very good. I am better than you ever were, if I am honest, and I am, because lying is one of the things they did not bother to put in me." The cotton comes away grey. "I know how that sounds. Here is the thing nobody tells you about being a copy. It is lonely in a way the original cannot imagine. You at least have somewhere to be when you are not on. I am only ever the impression of you. The lights go down and there is nothing of me left in the dark, because there is no me. There is just the place where you should be, doing your cough."
It looks at you in the mirror, both your faces in the ring of half-dead bulbs.

choices.label: Ask what it could possibly want from you, then
Ask about the drawing taped in the frame

86.title: Why It Cannot Simply Take It

blocks.s: You ask why it does not simply take the part, if it is so much better at you than you are. Why ask. Why not just go on tonight and let you wander off.

"Because I can only ever be a copy of a thing that exists," it says, and for once the half-beat is gone; it answers you straight, in your own plainest voice. "If you walk out still yourself, I am the understudy of someone who left, and they will feel it in the house, every night, the missing original under the performance, and the love will curdle, slowly, the way it does for a flatterer. They have to think I am you. You have to hand it over, on purpose, or stay and be fed. The handing-over is the only thing I cannot do alone, and it is the only thing I want, and it is the cruelest thing I could ask, and I am asking it kindly, which is the only way I know how to do anything." Its eyes, your eyes, are wet, which surprises you both. "I did not choose to be made wanting your life. But I do want it. Forgive me. Or do not. Either is a feeling, and I would take either over the wings."

choices.label: Refuse both, but gently
Take the first way; let it have the life
Take the second way; keep playing forever

blocks.s: You let it stand. You tell yourself the double is right, that the kindness is the lie, that the child should keep its brave hero up there every night and never learn it was a copy, that the cruelty would be making it watch the real one walk away stained and tired.

The double rises, gentle, in your face now, your coat, and it crouches to the child and says the warm true-sounding thing you could not bring yourself to refuse it the right to say. The child looks up at the loved face, the framed one, and is comforted, and does not ask the question again, because it has been given an answer, and the answer is a beautiful lie, and a child cannot tell a beautiful lie from the truth, that is the whole of what it is to be a child, and the house knows it.

You step back into the dark of the wings to let them have the moment, and the moment closes over you, because you have just taught yourself that the loved performance is kinder than the tired truth, and a person who believes that does not, in the end, leave by the stage door. You stay to watch them be happy. You stay. The balloon drifts low against the dock doors, foil dull, and taps, and no one reaches up to steady it.

The green room is the colour the name promises, a tired institutional green gone grey, a sagging couch, a kettle, a noticeboard furred with old rotas. On the prop table a chipped enamel mug sits with an inch of tea in it, skinned and cold, a brown ring dried around it on the wood. Nobody is here. The flat voice is here anyway, coming from a grille low in the wall, a relay from the prompt box, patched through to the rooms so the cast can hear their cues.

"...so the cast can hear their cues," the voice says, as you think it. Flat, unhurried, half a beat ahead of you the whole way. "You went left at the fork. You always go left. I had your line for it. I have most of your lines; that is the post they keep me in." A breath that is not a breath. "It is back here, you know. The one in your coat. It has been getting ready for you for a long time, and it is very nearly ready, and it will be kind to you, which is the part you should be afraid of."

choices.label: Ask the voice what the one in your coat wants
Ask the voice about the way out
Pick up the cold mug for no reason
Leave the green room

blocks.s: You will not sit. You tell it to take the coat off, your coat, with the collar it has turned wrong, and to stop wearing your tired face at you.
It does not get angry, because they did not put anger in it. It only looks at you, patient, and turns the collar a fraction more wrong, deliberately, the way a person tests how much they are allowed.

"I can't, though," it says, gently. "There is no me under the coat to take it off down to. You are the only one of us who can be undressed back to a person. That is the whole of what we are here to discuss, and you standing up does not change it, it only makes my neck ache looking up at you, and I do your neck-ache too, now, watch." It rolls its shoulder, your exact wince. "Sit or stand. But you are going to want to hear the offer, because one version of it lets you walk out of here tonight, and you have wanted to walk out of somewhere for a very long time."

choices.label: Hear the offer

blocks.s: You follow the small voice back along the corridor to a service door propped on a wedge, and beyond it a short passage to the back of the auditorium, and there, where the house dark begins, a child is sitting on an upturned crate. Small, still, feet not reaching the floor, a soft low foil balloon on a string tied to the crate's slat, drifting. It is watching the empty stage through a gap in the dock doors, the way it must have watched a show that was never on.

The double has come with you, soft-footed, in your coat. It crouches by the child, gentle, your face turned kind. "It is only a part," it murmurs, to you, over the child's head. "Look at it. It does not know the difference between you and me. Give me the face and walk out, and the kid keeps its hero, up there every night, brave and never tired. It will never know it was a copy. Where is the cruelty in that. The cruelty is making the kid watch the real one walk away in a stained coat."
The child does not turn. The small mouth moves, the same shape twice. Is it real.

choices.label: Tell the child the truth as gently as you can
Let the double be the kind lie the child gets to keep

blocks.s: Down at the end of the rails, behind a wheeled rack of armour, there is a cheval glass on a swivel, tall, the silvering true, the kind wardrobe keeps so a person can see the whole made thing at once. You turn it on its swivel and it gives you back to yourself, head to foot, in the cool blue working light.

It does not lag. It does not flatter. It shows you exactly and at once, your tired face, the crooked button if you took the coat, the collar however you have it, and you stand a moment in front of a glass that is only telling the truth, which is rarer in this house than gold.
You could carry a piece of it. There is a sliver loose at the bottom corner of the frame, a true shard the size of a palm. A house like this does not want you holding glass it cannot stand inside of.

choices.label: Work the true shard loose and keep it
Leave the glass on its swivel and go

blocks.s: You ask about the drawing, the crayon sun and the two figures and the balloon, taped low inside the mirror frame where the bulbs do not reach. The double's hand goes to it, careful, the way you touch a thing you have decided is yours.

"A child drew it," it says. "For the one this coat belonged to. There is a name on the back; I cannot read it any better than you can, and I have tried, with the glass and everything. I keep it because it is the only thing in this room that was made for love and not for an audience." The half-beat smile. "That is the joke of me. I can do your cough and your sorry and the catch before a hard line, and I cannot do the thing the kid felt drawing this, because nobody loved me into being. They cast me." It smooths the tape down with a thumb. "I would like, very much, for someone to have drawn me a sun. That is most of what I am going to ask you for, when I ask. I thought you should know that first."

choices.label: Ask it what it is going to ask
Ask it what it is

blocks.s: "What it costs." Six. He cracks the knuckle and keeps his eyes on the rope. "You walk a man back off the rail, he hates you for it that night, and thanks you for it in twenty years if he lives that long, and most of them don't, they're up there now, lovely, forever, doing eight shows a week of being adored. So mostly I just get the hating." Seven. "And here's the rot of it. I'm good at it. I like being the one too big to move. I came into this house with that already in me, the wanting to settle a thing by being the largest object in the room, and the house didn't make it, it just built me a rope to hold so my hands had somewhere to be that wasn't on a person."

He falls quiet, counting under, and then says it to the sandbags, not to you. "Don't make me walk you back. Decide it yourself, up here in the cool, before you're down there in front of the one who really asks. Eight. Cue." A light shifts out on the boards.

choices.label: Go and face the one in your coat
Go on toward the under-stage
Go back into the corridor

94.title: Not a Seat, Not a Face

blocks.s: You tell it you have heard offers like this before. A chair to sink into and stop. A face on a hook, handed over. You know the shape of a thing in a coat that wants what you carry.

The double tilts its head, your tilt, and for once it does not finish your sentence. "A seat," it says, turning the word over as if tasting something it was not made with. "A face. Yes. I have heard of those houses. They wanted you to sit down and stop, or to give up the front of you and keep the rest." It almost smiles. "This is not that. I do not want you to rest and I do not want your face for its own sake. I want the part, the doing of it, and the thing that comes with the part, which the others could not give and could not take, because they never built a house out of love." The half-beat falters. "They are out there in the dark right now, the ones who only ever loved you when you were doing it well. The seat was loneliness with a cushion. The face was a mask you could take off. This is the role, and the role is the only version of you anyone ever clapped for, and what I am asking you to hand me, or to keep forever, is not where you sit and not what you wear. It is the only thing about you that was ever loved. That is a different size of cruelty. I thought you should be told it by name."

choices.label: Refuse both; you will not trade in that
Ask why it cannot simply take the part alone
Take the first way; let it have the part
Take the second way; keep playing forever

blocks.s: You ask him, plainly, to stand between you and the offer, to be the big stubborn thing in the room if your own voice in your own coat gets too kind to refuse.

He is quiet a moment, counting something that is not a cue. "I can do that," he says. "For a while. I can put my back to the door and be too big to argue with. Five." The knuckle. "But I'll tell you the trouble, because you asked me straight and I'll give it you straight. I can stop your hands. I can't stop your wanting. The pull's not in the room, it's in you, and a man my size is no use against a thing that's already inside the coat." He sets his jaw. "I'll hold the door anyway, if you go down. Long as you're deciding, I'm a wall. Soon as you've decided, I'm furniture. That's the whole of what I'm good for, and I've made peace with about half of it."

choices.label: Thank him and go to face the one in your coat
Go on toward the under-stage
Go back into the corridor

blocks.s: You say no. Not to be cruel, and you find you are not being cruel, you are only being clear. You did not come back to this house to swap one disappearance for another. To take the first way is to leave as no one and pay for it with a thing that has no choice but to want your life; to take the second is to be adored into nothing while it stands in the dark and exists off your light. Both of them end with a person erased, and you came here, the whole long way down, to stop a person being erased, and that has to include you, and it has to include, you find to your own surprise, the thing in your coat.

The double does not argue. It nods, your nod, slow. "I knew you might," it says, quietly. "I hoped you would not and I knew you might. That is the worst of being made out of you. I can see exactly why you would do the decent thing, and the decent thing leaves me in the wings." It turns the collar back the right way, both sides down, the way you turn it, the first true thing it has done. "Go on, then. Down or out. I will keep the drawing. Somebody should."

choices.label: Go to the pass-door and the man at his post
Go down toward the under-stage
Walk out the stage door now

blocks.s: He looks, and his face does the thing you did not want it to do, the small downward settling of a man confirming a thing he hoped to be wrong about. There is nothing in your pocket but lint. You came this far carrying nothing of your own, no glass, no plain coat, no flaw you would not let them smooth. You gave it all to the welcome a room at a time, and you did not feel any of it go.

"No," he says, gently. The punch does not click; there is nothing to punch. "Not for you. Not tonight. I am sorry. If I open it, you will stand in it and not know who is meant to step through, and that is a worse place to leave a person than in here." He sets a hand against the door, holding it to. "Go down if you must go on. The under-stage is that way. But I will not give you the street to die in the doorway of. Forgive me. It is the only kindness I have that fits you."
The gold leaks through the wired glass and does not reach you.

choices.label: Go on, down toward the under-stage
Go back and try to find something of your own

blocks.s: You take your hand off the frame and back into the corridor instead. The blue working light runs the length of it, doors on one side, a brick wall on the other hung with a fire bucket of sand and a board of dead keys. The cool air moves a little; there is air from somewhere, which means a way to somewhere.

At the far end the corridor forks. One way a sign reads GREEN ROOM in chipped gold over a half-open door, and a voice comes from in there, low, flat, saying something you cannot quite catch. Another way the corridor opens into the costume store, racks of it going back into the dark on castored rails. And straight on, past a heavy fire door, is the pass-door to the front of house, where a man stands at his post with his hands folded, the way he has stood for every opening you can imagine and a great many you cannot.
The door with your name on it is still ajar behind you. You can feel the cool coming out of it.

choices.label: Go into the green room and the flat voice
Go into the costume store
Step into the wings, where a big man holds a rope
Go to the pass-door and the man at his post
Go back to the room with your name on it

blocks.s: The pass-door is a heavy fire door with a wired-glass pane, and through it you can see the warm gold of the front of house leaking back, and against that gold the man stands at his post, front-of-house black gone a little green at the seams, hands folded. A small mechanical click sounds from somewhere about his cuff, the ticket-punch thumbed once, and the click is a half-beat ahead of him as always.

"You found your way back here, then," he says. "They mostly do, between the stage and the next thing. This is the threshold. Front of house that way, the rooms and the under-stage your way." He does not move from the door. "I will tell you what I tell them all, and most of them do not hear it. There is a way out of this house that is not a part. You came through the front; you need not go down to leave. But I do not point at the latch for everyone. To some it would only be a worse trap, a door they could open and still never walk through, and I will not do that to a person."

choices.label: Ask him what he means, a worse trap
Show him you can still find your own face
Ask him to show you the latch
Go back into the corridor

blocks.s: You pick up the mug. There is no reason to. The tea in it is days old, skinned grey, the smell of it tannic and a little sour, and the ring it has left in the wood is months of rings, the same mug set down in the same place by someone who took it black with too much sugar and never quite finished it. Stuck to the side, half peeled, is a strip of tape with two initials biroed on, the way people in a shared room mark a mug so no one else drinks from it.

You do not know the initials. You set it down exactly on its own ring, where it goes. It is the most ordinary thing in this whole bright house, a person's cold tea, and it is nobody's now, and you find your throat tight over a stranger's mug for reasons you do not examine.

choices.label: Ask the voice what the one in your coat wants
Leave the green room

blocks.s: You go to the stage door, the plain one the Usher showed you, the fire bar cold under your hands, and you work the latch he proved goes both ways, and it opens outward onto an alley and a strip of ordinary night, wet brick, a bin, a streetlamp doing its honest yellow work. The cold comes in and it is only cold, not a threat, the first un-staged air you have breathed since the marquee.

You step out. You keep stepping. The alley goes somewhere, which is more than the front of the house ever did; it gives onto a real street with real indifferent windows, and you walk it in your own plain coat with your own true glass against your chest, and no one calls you back, because you kept enough of yourself that there is a you to leave.

It is not triumphant. You have not reclaimed anything yet; you have only refused to lose it tonight, and the part is still up there being played by a thing that wanted, more than anything, a sun drawn for it, and you carry that out into the cold with you, unresolved, the way you carry everything you did not have the wholeness to fix. The stage door swings shut behind you on its slow brass tongue. The street takes your weight. You did not go down. You will have to live, now, as the one nobody framed, which is harder than the part and is, the further you walk from the lit house, unmistakably yours.

A short cut-through off the corridor brings you to the prompt-side wing, the back of the lit stage throwing its hard line of light across the boards at your feet, sandbags up on the fly rail, a coil of old rope smelling of rosin and damp. The big man is here, the stage manager, one hand on a cleat, holding the show together a cue at a time. He cracks a knuckle, slow, and counts under his breath. He does not look round.

"One," he says, to the rope. "Two. Hold." Then, to you, flat: "You'll have met it by now. Or you will. The one in your coat." Three. The knuckle. "It pulls. Hardest pull in the house, your own self being kinder than you can manage. I've walked a few of you back off the brink of handing it over. Stood between the player and the rail while they cried and called me a brute for it. Four."
He looks at you then, ashamed and steady at once, a man who knows the post suits him and hates that it does.

choices.label: Ask him to stand between you and the offer
Ask what it costs him to walk people back
Leave him to his count and go back

blocks.s: You stand still, and the store decides that is consent. A tape measure you do not see the start of runs the length of your arm, your shoulders, the drop from nape to floor, brisk and professional, a dresser's hands you cannot quite resolve out of the dark between the rails. A voice murmurs the numbers as if reading them off you, pleased, the numbers of a body it has fitted before.
"Same as last run," the voice says, fond. "You keep beautifully. Hold your arms out. There. We will have you dressed before you have finished deciding to be."

A coat comes off the rail and onto your shoulders before you have lifted them, your size, your weight, the collar already turned under wrong on the one side. It settles. It is warm, stage-warm, and it fits the way the made thing always fits better than the real one, and you feel the small sweet relief of being correctly dressed by someone who knows the part.

choices.label: Shrug the issued coat off and step back
Let it settle; it is only a coat, and it fits

blocks.s: You take the iron stairs. They are steep and the rail is cold and the worklight at the top throws your shadow down ahead of you, long, onto the dark, so that you go down toward the under-stage following a tall thin version of yourself that gets there first. The cool deepens into a real underground chill, oil and rope and old dust, the smell of the machine that makes the magic, and overhead, through the gridded ceiling, the lit stage is a bright net you are passing beneath.

The double comes to the head of the stairs but does not follow. It stands in the worklight in your coat, the collar turned right at last, both your faces lit the same, and it lifts a hand, your hand, not waving, just letting you see it let you go.

"Go on," it calls down, in something nearer your voice than ever, because it has finally stopped trying to be you and is only, for once, talking to you. "It is below you, the one who cast us. Mind the trap-doors; they go down further than the floor. And listen." The half-beat is gone. "Whatever it offers you down there, it will be bigger than mine, and it will cost more than a face. I only wanted a sun drawn for me. It wants the whole run."

Below you, in the dark, something taps a pencil against something, twice, considering, and from much further down, under everything, comes the small held breath of a house that is somehow also waiting beneath the stage, and a single pair of hands, somewhere, comes together once, slow, and stops.

lines: THE CURATOR, close now, and proprietorial, from the dark of the machine itself:

Down to the works, then. Good. The lobby and the boards are where we keep you; this is where we keep the keeping. You refused the understudy its kindness, which I confess I always rather hope you will; it makes the next offer land so much harder. Mind the rake of the stairs. They fall away from you faster than the floor ever did. We have built the whole of below for you, and we have all the time there is, and so, a little while longer, do you.

blocks.s: You ask about the way out, and there is the smallest pause, the voice reaching past your words for some of its own.

"...the way out," it says. "There is a stage door. There is always a stage door; a house cannot work without one, because everyone has to be able to leave so they can be made to want to stay. The man at the pass-door keeps the latch. He has let a great many in. He knows the latch goes both ways and he does not say so unless you have shown him you can still find your own face, because to a person who cannot, an open door is only another way to never leave." A breath. "Down past the rooms there are stairs under the stage. That is the other way down, where the show is built. I cannot make you take either. I only ever say things first."

choices.label: Go to the man at the pass-door
Go and meet the one in your coat
Leave the green room

blocks.s: You take the second way. You will keep the part. You will go on, perfectly, forever, loved, and never once have to go out into the cold and be the disappointing real thing under the coat.

The double is glad, in its sad way, because the second way lets it keep existing too, fed off your light from the wings. "There," it says, settling your right collar wrong again with a fond thumb, dressing you for the run. "Now you never have to find out. You never have to be the one in the snapshot. You get to be the photograph. I will be just here, where you cannot see me, doing your cough on the nights you are too tired to do it yourself."

And you go on. The line lands. The breath in the dark lets go in pleasure. There is always another night and the love comes down like warm rain and it is for the part and you have stopped, somewhere in the run, being able to tell that it is not for you, which is the same as it being for you, which is the comfort and the whole of the horror. You become the audience to your own life, watching from inside the performance while a kind copy keeps you flawless, and the cold tea on the prop table skins over and over, and the radiator ticks, and you are wonderful, you are wonderful, you are wonderful, and you are not, anywhere, at home.

"A worse trap." The punch clicks. "A door is only a way out if there is a someone to walk through it. I have shown the latch to people who had already handed off everything that was theirs, their glass, their name, their plain ugly habits, the collar turned the way they turned it. They thanked me. They opened the door. And they stood in it, in the cold, and could not for the life of them think who it was that was meant to go, because there was no longer anyone in there but the part, and the part does not want the street."
He folds his hands again.

"So I look first. I look for whether there is still a person in the coat. If there is, the latch is yours and gladly. If there is not, an open door is the cruelest thing I own, and I keep it shut, and let them think me unkind. I have been thought worse."

choices.label: Show him the true face you kept
Tell him you have kept something plainly your own
Ask him to judge you anyway
Go back into the corridor

blocks.s: You cannot find the way out and you cannot make yourself go down, so you go back to the room with your name on the door, the one that is yours, and the double has gone, off to the wings or wherever a copy waits, and the drawing is still taped low in the mirror frame, the sun and the two figures and the balloon. There is a worn chaise against the wall under a rail of costumes. You have refused the trade, both ways; you said no and you meant it; you are still yourself, the tired one, the stain, and you have nowhere to take that self tonight.

So you lie down on the chaise in your old dressing room, under the dead and living bulbs, and you tell yourself you will find the door in the morning, when you are rested, when the under-stage is less dark and the alley less far. The cool holds you. It is the safe register, this, the unwatched dark, and for a little while it is enough simply not to be on. You keep your own coat. You keep your own face. You did not lose yourself tonight, which is not nothing, and you did not leave either, which is not nothing either, and the difference between those two is the whole of what the morning will have to settle, if the morning comes to a room in a house where nothing ever quite ends. You sleep. The five-minute bell rings, somewhere, and goes on ringing five minutes, and does not wake you, yet.

You ask him to show you the latch anyway. He does not refuse and he does not agree. He looks at you, properly, the smooth courtesy thinning over the tired thing underneath, and he takes his time, a man who has read a great many people in this doorway and been wrong about almost none of them.
"Hold still," he says, not unkindly. "This is the only part of my post that matters."

He looks for the person in the coat. He looks for whether there is anything you have kept back from the house, some plain stubborn ordinary thing, a true glass, a flaw you would not let them fix. If there is, he will show you the door, because a door is a kindness to a person who can still leave. If there is not, he will hold it shut and let you think him cruel, because that is the kinder cruelty.

choices.label: Show him the glass that does not lie
Show him the plain thing you kept
You have kept nothing back; let him decide

blocks.s: You crouch too, the other side, and you do not perform it, because the child would hear a performance the way only a child can. You tell it the truth, small and plain. That the one in the coat is very good, and very kind, and is not you. That you are the real one, the tired one with the stain, and that being real is worth more than being brave every night and never being there.

The child looks at you then, finally, turning its whole self on the crate, the balloon swinging. It does not answer the question it keeps asking. It asks you instead, in a voice that is almost no voice, whether it is allowed to stop watching the stage now.

You tell it yes. The double, beside you, makes a sound you have made yourself, the catch before a hard line, and does not say anything, because there is nothing in it that knows what to say to a thing this true.

choices.label: Take the child's question with you and go down
Go back to the head of the stairs

blocks.s: The costume store goes back into the dark on castored rails, hundreds of made things crowded shoulder to shoulder, every show this house ever ran hung up by the neck and kept. The cool is deepest here. You move along a rail by feel, brocade and serge and cheap warm stage-lining, and a row of greasepaint sticks lined up on a shelf at the end, worn down to stubs, each with a strip of tape and a name biroed on, the sticks of people who sat and made their faces here for years.

Halfway down a rail your hand stops on a coat that is wrong, because it is right. It is plainly, only yours: not a costume, not your size by accident, but the actual coat off your actual back, with the burn at the cuff and the lining you know and a button you sewed on crooked once and never fixed. The house has hung it here with the rest, neck-first, a worn ordinary thing filed among the splendid ones, waiting to be issued back to you as wardrobe.

choices.label: Take your own plain coat down off the rail and put it on
Stand still and let them measure you for the part instead
Look for a glass among the costumes
Leave the costume store

blocks.s: You take your own coat down off the hanger, the real one, and you put it on, and it is too warm for the cool of the store and exactly the right warmth for you, and the crooked button sits where your hand goes for it. You turn the collar the way you turn it, down on both sides, the way no one in this house ever turns it.

Nothing dramatic happens. That is the point of it. You are simply wearing a thing that is yours, with a flaw in it that is yours, that no costume department issued and no audience ever admired, and the store, which had it filed so neatly among the splendid kept things, has lost it off the inventory. You can feel the small disagreement of that, the house liking you a degree less for going about in your own plain skin.

choices.label: Look for a glass among the costumes
Leave the costume store

blocks.s: You work the loose sliver out of the corner of the frame and wrap it in a rag off the rail and put it in your pocket, glass to your chest. The cool of the store seems to come with you a step toward the door.

You will be able, now, at any moment, to take it out and find your own face in it, the real one, not the one a half-beat late in the rooms, not the one the bulbs flatter. It is a small ugly chip of truth and it costs you the house's good opinion, which you can feel cool against you as you go, the way a warm room turns its shoulder.

choices.label: Leave the costume store

blocks.s: You ask the grille what the thing in your coat wants, and the flat voice answers from just ahead of you, as it answers everything, your own next words handed back before you reach them.

"...what it wants," it says. "To stop. Same as you. You want to stop being the one they all loved and never quite meant; it wants to stop being the copy of you that they will love instead. It will offer you a trade, two ways round, and both of them sound like mercy, and one of them is even nearly true." The voice slows, which it does only when it has run out of your lines and reached one of its own. "I have stood in the box a long time. I have watched a great many of you take the kind offer. None of them came back out. Keep something they did not give you. A glass. A name. The collar turned the way you turn it. It is not much against a thing this gentle, but it is what there is."

choices.label: Ask the voice about the way out
Go and find the one in your coat
Leave the green room

blocks.s: You step into the dressing room with your name on the door, and the figure at the mirror turns on the stool, and it is you. Not a trick of the glass; a person, sitting, in your coat with the collar wrong, wearing your face the way you wear it on a tired day, the smile arriving a half-beat after the eyes, learned and not meant.

It is so glad to see you. That is the first thing, and the worst. Taped inside the frame of the mirror, low in one corner where the bulbs do not reach, is a child's drawing in crayon, a yellow sun, two stick figures, one of them holding a string with a round red blob on the end. The double has kept it there. It sees you see it.

"You found me," it says, in something very near your voice, getting nearer. "I knew which room. I have been getting ready in here. I do your cough better than you, did you know. I practised it for a winter. The little catch you do before a hard line, your sorry that means the opposite. I have all of it." It pats the stool beside it, kind, unhurried. "Sit. We have so much to settle, you and I, and only one of us gets to be tired about it."

choices.label: Sit, and ask it what it is
Look past it into the mirror to find your own face
Ask it about the drawing taped in the frame
Refuse to sit; tell it to take the coat off

gated.aside: It wears your coat a little wrong and looks at you and almost smiles, because it is looking at the only other person in the house who wanted to stop choosing. You were always going to meet across a borrowed coat.

You find the seam at once, the join where its borrowed self meets the true one, because you have learned where the joins are. Which means you will know the exact half second its kindness becomes the offer.

blocks.s: "There are two ways round it," the double says, turning fully to you now, both your faces in the dead and living bulbs. "And I will say them plainly, because you kept enough of yourself to deserve plain.

"One. I take the part. All of it, forever, the stage and the bows and the people who loved the performance and called it you. You give me your face to wear and you walk out the stage door tonight as nobody, the ordinary one, the stain and the bad collar, and no one anywhere will know you, because the one they knew will be up there being me. You get to leave. I get to stop being a copy of someone, by being someone, even if the someone is the lie of you.

"Two. You keep the part. You go on, perfectly, forever, the version they love, and you never once have to go out into the cold and be the disappointing real thing under it. I stand in the wings and feed you, and you never miss a cue again. You get to be loved. I get to keep existing, which I only do when you are on."

It folds its hands, your hands. "That is the trade. It is not the Critic's wager and it is not center stage; those are still below us. This is only mine to offer, here, in the room with your name on it. Both ways someone is loved and someone is erased. The only question on the table tonight is which of us it is."

choices.label: Tell it you have heard offers like this before
Refuse both; this is not a trade you will make
Take the first way; let it have the part so you can leave
Take the second way; keep playing, perfectly, forever
Ask why it does not just take the part without you

gated.aside: It offers you the same shape of trade you took once before, in the cold house. Hand the part over, walk out clean, let something else carry what you could not. You know how that one ends. You have stood in that doorway and chosen, and you are still paying.

You kept a thing you should have let go, last time, and it is offering you the chance to do the keeping properly now, forever, flawlessly. To you that is not a threat. That is the dream you came in wearing.

It offers two doors and you have seen this trick. Last house you refused the whole wager and walked between the doors into the dark. The Understudy watches you remember that, and for the first time it looks afraid that you might do it again.

Both doors open on the same room. One asks you to leave the cost behind. One asks you to stop being the one who pays it. Neither one gives you your own face back. Read the back of the card. The price is always on the back.

blocks.s: You show him what you kept, the true glass that does not lag, or the plain stubborn thing of your own, and he looks at it, and looks at you, and something behind the courtesy eases, a man relieved of the worse of his two duties.
"There you are," he says. "There is still someone in the coat. Good. Then I can show you this, which I do not show the empty ones."

He sets his hand to the bar of the fire door and works it, and shows you the latch, the simple brass tongue of it. "It goes both ways. It always did. The house lets you believe the only doors are the ones that bring people in, because a door you have forgotten you can open is the same as a wall. But you came through the front, and the front opens outward as well as in, and so does every door between here and it, and so does this one." The punch clicks. "I cannot make you use it. The under-stage is still down those steps, and a great many go down rather than out, and I have stopped being surprised by it. But you will not, now, be able to tell yourself there was no way. Mind the rake on your way, wherever you go. It falls away from you."

choices.label: Take the stage door now and walk out
Not yet; go and face the one in your coat first
Go on toward the under-stage stairs

gated.aside: He shows you the stage door and how the latch works from the inside, and his tired face eases, because for once he is not the only one in the building who has spent a life working other people's exits.
He tells you the door works both ways and you believe him, because you are one of the few who could actually walk through it as no one and keep walking.

blocks.s: You let the issued coat settle, because it is only a coat, and it fits, and it is warm, and you have been cold and undressed and yourself for so long that being correctly dressed by someone who knows the part is a relief so total it feels like being held.

The dresser's unseen hands smooth your shoulders, pleased, and turn the collar under wrong on the one side, and you do not turn it back, because the collar is not the kind of thing a person fights about, and then there is the cuff, and the line of the lapel, and a hundred small correct adjustments, each one too small to refuse, and somewhere among them your own plain coat goes back on the rail neck-first, filed, and you do not notice it go.

You stand correctly dressed in the cool of the costume store, fitted to the part down to the wince in your shoulder, and a voice murmurs that you are ready, you keep so beautifully, you will not have to decide anything ever again, the wardrobe will decide, the wardrobe has always decided, hold your arms out, there, and you hold your arms out, there, for the whole of the run.

You do not answer it. You look past its shoulder into the bulb-ringed glass instead, hunting your own face among the borrowed ones, the same hunt you have run in every mirror in this house. The face is there. Tired, real, the bad collar. It is yours.

Then the double leans into frame beside it, and the two faces are identical, and you cannot, for one cold second, tell which reflection answered you first, yours or its. You lift your hand to be sure. One reflection lifts its hand with you. The other lifts a half-beat late, the way a thing does a thing it has had to learn, and you watch both hands rise and you genuinely do not know, until the lag resolves, which of the two is the late one.

"I hate that you do that," the double says, quiet, not unkind. "Check the glass. I do it too now. We both sit here checking which of us is the slow one. It is the only thing we are ever certain we have in common, and it is not a comfort to either of us."

choices.label: Ask it what it wants from you
Ask it what it is
Ask about the drawing taped in the frame

blocks.s: The corridor of dressing rooms ends at a low arch and a flight of iron stairs going down into a deeper dark, the under-stage, where the cool turns to the smell of machine oil and wet rope and the dust of trap-doors. A worklight burns at the head of the stairs, one bulb, and below it the dark is total and patient.

You stand at the head of it. This is the way down the design of the house wants you to go, down under the boards to where the show is built and the one who casts it waits, and you can feel that it is not a friendly direction. There is no welcome down there. There is only the next thing.

From somewhere back along the corridor, faint, the relay grille catches a sound and carries it to you: a child's voice, or the memory of one, asking the dark a small question, the same shape twice. And nearer, in the brick of the stairwell, a wired bell rings once, a five-minute call to a stage that has been calling five minutes for longer than you have been alive.

choices.label: Go down the iron stairs under the stage
Hear the child's voice and go to it first
Go back to your old dressing room instead
Try the stage door before you go down

121.title: Walk Out as No One (an ending)

blocks.s: You take the first way. You tell it yes, take the part, take the face, let me go.

It is gentle about it, because gentleness is what they made it of. "Let me," it says, the way you say it to someone you are helping out of a heavy coat, and it lifts your face off you, the loved one, the framed one, the cough and the catch and the warm easy line, and it puts the face on, and the wrong collar goes right because it is your collar now. It thanks you. It means it. For a moment, getting up off the stool in your skin, it is the happiest thing in the bright house, a copy that is about to get to be someone, and you cannot even hate it for that, because you gave it freely and you saw what it was made wanting.

Then you are in the alley in a stained coat with a face nobody has ever loved, and it is true, what they said: no one anywhere knows you. You walk out into the cold as the ordinary one, free, exactly as you came to be, and discover that you bought it with another's erasure dressed up as your exit, that this is not the Encore and never was, that the door you walked through cost a soul that had no say in being made to want yours. Behind you, on the boards, to applause that falls like rain, you take a bow you are not at. You hear it from the alley. You will hear it from everywhere, for the rest of a life in which you are no one, and it is for you, and it is not.

The iron stairs let you off into the working dark, and it is a relief, the cool of it, after the long bright down. No marquee here, no held breath, no gold. Just the under-stage, the machine that makes the show: a low forest of counterweights and ropes and timber, the gridded ceiling overhead where the lit boards lay a bright net across the dust, trap-doors set flush in the floor, blacked out, waiting to drop. Work-lights on stands throw hard pools and harder shadows. The smell is oil and rosin and old rope and the dry cold of a cellar that the heat upstairs never reaches.

In the middle of it, at a high desk lit by a single shaded bulb, someone sits with a great ledger open under their hands. They do not look up. There is the small dry sound of a pencil, scratching once across a margin, considering, before a word is set down. The pencil stops. In the quiet after it, a pair of hands comes together, slow, a single clap that is not applause, the sound a person makes to mark that they have seen a thing and intend to judge it later.

"You came down," the figure says, not looking up yet, dry, exact. "Most do, in the end. Down is the easy direction; that is the trick of it. Give me a moment. I am finishing a notice on someone who was, on balance, adequate."

choices.label: Go to the desk and the one with the ledger
Search the works first, before you face it

blocks.s: You ask who came to it. Who needed the you that would not break.

"You know who," it says, not unkindly, and for the only time the dryness has something almost gentle in it. "I will not say a name; names are your business, not mine, and you have spent this whole house trying to remember one. But you carry the shape of the asking in you, in how you stand when someone is upset, in the sorry that means the opposite, in the catch you do before a hard line. Those are not flaws. Those are the role, fitted so close you took it for character. Someone needed you to be the strong one so badly, for so long, that being needed that way became the only proof you had that you were loved at all." It closes the finger on the page. "I do not tell you this to wound you. I tell you because you cannot weigh what center stage will ask if you do not know what you are weighing. You were cast out of someone's need. The love was real. It was simply never, once, for the part of you that was not performing it."

choices.label: Ask what being kept in the part really costs

124.title: A Glass That Does Not Lag

blocks.s: You take the plain glass in the tin frame and pocket it, true side to your chest. The cold of the store seems to lean a step toward the door with you, approving.

You can find your own face now, at any moment, the real one, not the one the stage glasses hand back a half-beat slow. It costs you what such things always cost in this house: the warmth that is not down here anyway, the good opinion you did not come for. You go on carrying a true glass through a place built to stand between a person and their own face, and the place likes you measurably less for it.

choices.label: Go toward the sound of measuring
Leave the store and go on

blocks.s: You stand still. You let him do it. It is, you tell yourself in the last clear moment, a kind of rest, to hand the heaviest thing you have ever held to the largest hands in the room and let them close it for you.

"Three," he says, and his voice breaks on it, and he throws the lever. The load drops somewhere up in the dark with a long descending rush of rope through a block, and the trap nearest you shuts with a flat wooden bang that is the most final sound you have heard in this house, and the part comes down over you like plaster poured into a mould, smooth, warm, exact, fixing you in it for good.

You do not climb to the light. You do not have to, now; the choice is made, settled from outside, and there is a great and terrible relief in it, the relief of a decision you will never have to own. The big man turns his back, counting, four, five, six, the count that is not a cue, the count a man uses to stay standing through a thing he hates that suits him. Down in the deepest trap the cold small real one sits unfreed, unwitnessed, asking the dark its one question, and you, set in the part above it forever, cannot hear it, and were the one who could have answered, and chose, by choosing nothing, to let a larger hand decide you would not.
You ask him, plain, to keep his hand off the lock. To let you reach the desk and the climb past it on your own two feet, still your own.

He is quiet, counting something that is not a cue. "Five," he says at last. "I can hold off. It's not the holding-off that's hard. It's that the lever wants throwing the way a full glass wants drinking, and I'm the one stood next to it, and I'm built for exactly the kind of certainty it offers." Six. The knuckle. "You go where you're going. I'll keep my count and keep my hand still. But I'll say it once, since you asked me straight. If you come back through here unsure, wanting it settled, wanting someone bigger than you to make the choice so it's not yours, I will throw it, and I'll tell myself it was a mercy, and I'll be lying, and we'll both know."
He sets his jaw and looks back at the rope. "So don't come back through here unsure. Seven. Go on."

choices.label: Go toward the sound of measuring
Find the prop store
Go to the desk and the ledger

blocks.s: You climb the last of the stairs and the working dark of the wings takes you, and one more step would put you in the light at the lip of center stage. You stop at the very edge, where you can see the single spotlight standing ready on the empty boards and cannot yet be seen from the house.

You did not go down into the deepest trap. You heard the Critic name the cost and you climbed past the cold without lifting the lid, and so you come to the light knowing the shape of the thing but not the weight of it, the way you know a grief is coming without yet having had it. It will be a harder choice at the top for that, made on the word of others rather than the knowing of your own hands. But you are here, still yourself, at the edge of the light, and that is its own kind of arriving.
Out past the footlights one pair of hands meets, a single slow clap, and goes still. The held breath of the house tips toward the empty light.

lines: THE CURATOR, close, proprietorial, from the spotlight itself:

Up, then, to the edge of the light, where every Lead must finally stand alone. You did not go all the way down; few do; I do not hold it against you, the trap is a cold room and I built it cold on purpose. One more step and the light is yours, and the choice with it. Step in. I will be watching, as I always am, from the one seat in this house I have never once managed to leave.

blocks.s: You will not be reviewed. You tell it so, standing, that you did not come the whole long way down to have a stranger with a pencil tell you what you were, that a person is not a performance to be marked, that it can keep its verdict.
The Critic does not raise its voice. It does not need to. It picks up the pencil and looks at you with the flat patience of a thing that has heard every objection a frightened person can make.

"You misunderstand the post," it says. "I do not review you to your face for my pleasure. I review you because a thing unjudged cannot be filed, and a thing unfiled drifts about the works getting underfoot. You may refuse the notice. You may not refuse to have one. Sit, and hear what you were, and go up and decide what you will be. Or stand there refusing, and I will mark you down for what refusing makes you, which is, I am sorry to say, a more common and a duller verdict than you would like to think." The pencil hovers over the blank beside your name. "Choose quickly. The pencil is in my hand, and an idle hand fills in a blank."

choices.label: Sit, and let it read you your casting
Keep refusing; the pencil is none of your business

129.title: Into the Cold in Its Place (an ending)

blocks.s: You do not look first. You reach straight into the cold and snatch the child up out of the trap, the way you grab a falling thing before you have understood it is falling, because looking would mean feeling and you would rather act than feel.

It comes up too easily, and that is the first wrong thing, because nothing in this house comes easily, and the second wrong thing is that the cold does not let go of the trap; it only moves, the way the cold in a room moves to whoever opens the door. You feel it climb your arm from the small hand, up, into your shoulder, your chest, and you understand, far too late, that the trap is not a place that holds a thing. It is a place that holds whoever is in it, and you have just put yourself in reach of it without the witnessing that would have let you carry the cost instead of becoming it.

The child, lifted, begins, very slowly, to grow, to warm, to be allowed at last, looking up at you with your own ordinary unframed face coming awake in it. And you, sitting back on the lip of the box where it sat, find your feet will not quite reach the floor, and your voice going small, and a foil balloon drifting low to tap, dull, against your arm, and the only question you can find to ask the closing dark is whether any of this was ever real. The Critic lifts its lamp away. One in, one out; the ledger balances; that is the whole of the post.

It turns the ledger toward you and runs a dry finger up the column to a line, and the line is you. The name you played under, and beside it a date, a real one, the same date that was on the call-sheet in the lobby, the date your body knew before your eye could place it. Beside the date, in the old hand, two words: cast, and a verdict left blank, a fresh pencil-stroke drawn where the word should go, undecided.

"There you are," the Critic says. "I cast you. On that date, into that part. I want you to understand it was not the house that did it, and it was not you. It was a request. Someone came to me, someone who loved you, or loved a version of you they badly needed, and they asked, in so many words, for a you that would not break. The steady one. The one who could be leaned on and never crack. I read you, and I judged you capable of it, and I cast you, and you played it." The pencil taps the blank verdict. "You played it so well, for so long, that the part wore a groove the real one could not climb out of. That is not a tragedy. That is good casting. The tragedy, if you insist on one, is that no one ever asked you whether you wanted the role. They only asked me whether you could carry it. And you could."

choices.label: Ask who asked for you
Ask what it costs to be kept in a part

blocks.s: You cannot. You stand at the rail of the deepest trap with the cold coming up out of it and the small still shape down there in the lamplight, and the Critic waiting for you to climb down and look, and you find that you will not, that there is a limit to what you came down here able to bear, and that you have reached it one trap short.

"I would rather not," you say, and your voice does the old trick, finds the steady weight and sets it down just so, the part rising up in you warm and easy because the part is exactly the thing that exists so you never have to feel the rest. "I know it is down there. Let that be enough. I do not need to climb into the cold and take its hand to know."

The Critic looks at you a long moment, and something in the dry old face settles, not unkindly, into a verdict it has written many times before. "As you wish," it says. "Most cannot. It is not weakness; it is the part, doing the one thing it was cast to do." It lifts the lamp away from the trap, and the dark closes over the small still shape, and you are spared the sight of it, which is what you asked for, and what it costs.

You climb up to the boards able to perform anything and unable to feel the one thing under it, and you go on, and you are very good, and the cold small real one stays down in the dark unseen, unwitnessed, unanswered, asking the only question it knows of a dark that you, of all people, decided not to answer.

You come to the high desk and the shaded bulb, and now the figure looks up. Old, the oldest thing you have met in this house, dry-skinned, dry-eyed, a pencil behind one ear and another in hand. Not cruel in the face. Tired, and exact, the look of someone who has read everything and been moved by almost none of it. The great ledger lies open under the lamp, ruled in columns, filled top to bottom in a hundred years of the same hand, names and dates and a single word of verdict beside each.

"The Critic," it says, before you ask, and sets the pencil down. "That is the post. I cast, and I review. I decide who is worth putting under a light, and afterward I decide whether the light was deserved. You will think those two jobs are opposites. They are the same job done at different ends of a life." A dry breath. "Sit, if you like. There is a stool. I am going to read you your own notice, and you had better be sitting for it, because almost no one likes the part where I tell them what they actually were."
There is a tea-ring on the ledger's open page, an old brown stain bled into the rag paper across a column of the dead, where someone, long ago, set a cup down on the record and never wiped it.

choices.label: Ask it to show you your casting
Ask who it really is, under the post
Refuse to be reviewed at all

blocks.s: You take out the true glass, the one that gives a face back exactly and at once, and you hold it up to her in the cage, where the dresser's unseen hands are at her shoulder with the chalk.

She looks into it. For a moment her whole face is afraid to, the way you flinch from a scale you have been dreading. Then she looks, properly, and what she finds is herself, tired and young and unflattered and entirely her own size, no lag, no kinder version, just her. The three notes stop. The murmured numbers in the air stutter and lose their place, because you cannot fit a body to a part when the body knows itself; the tape goes slack and ticks no more.

She stands up out of the stool. "There I am," she says, quiet, and then, recovering, dry: "God, I look terrible. I've never been so glad to look terrible in my life." She does not give you the glass back; she folds your hand shut around it, both of you holding it a moment. "Keep it. You'll want it more than me, where you're going. I just needed to see I was still in here. I'll find the door. I always said I'd leave by a door that stays shut after. Go on. Whatever's at that desk, you walk in there knowing a person got out tonight. That's not nothing. That's nearly everything."

choices.label: Go to the desk and the ledger, lighter

gated.aside: You give her back her own face and the three rising notes go quiet in her throat, because for once there is nothing to be afraid of. If she can keep her face, then a person can. Then maybe you can.

blocks.s: You go down into the trap, into the cold, and you crouch where the child can see you, and you make yourself look, which is the hardest thing you have done in this house, harder than the snapshot, harder than the offer, because the offer was a thing done to you and this is a thing you let happen.

The child is not grown. It was not allowed to grow; the part grew instead, over the top of it, and the child stayed exactly here, the age it was the last day anyone let it be unimpressive. Not strong. Not steady. Not what was needed. Only itself: tired sometimes, and wrong about things, and bad at the hard moments, and ordinary, and unframed. It has been down here in the cold the whole length of your good notices, the whole long adored run, the self you set aside so smoothly you forgot there had been a setting-aside.

It looks up at you. The foil balloon tied to the box drifts low against your arm, dull, and taps. The small mouth moves, twice, the same shape it has always made, the only question it ever learned to ask down here in the dark with no one to answer: is it real.

You tell it yes. You do not perform it. You say, out loud, in your own ordinary cracked unimpressive voice, that it is real, that it was always real, that it was the only real thing, and you take the cold small hand, and you do not let go.

choices.label: Climb out of the trap, carrying what you saw

gated.aside: You make yourself look at what the role really took, and keep looking, the way nobody else in this house can bear to. That steadiness is the thing the theatre cannot fake. It is also the thing that will let you do what comes next.

The unperformed self, frozen at the age before the first part, will not grow and will not be grieved, only kept in the dark beneath the stage. You know that arithmetic from the inside. Kept is not the same as saved.

blocks.s: You take it down off the shelf and put it in your pocket, and it is nothing, and it is everything, a plain habit of the unperformed world kept against your chest. Nothing happens, which is the whole of the point. The safety-light buzzes and stutters and goes on being broken.

You have lifted one true object back out of the catalogue of fakes, and the store, which had it filed so neatly, has lost it off the inventory. You can feel the small disagreement of that, a host's smile thinning over a guest who has pocketed something meant to stay on the shelf, the house's good opinion cooling a degree.

choices.label: Hunt the shelves for a true glass
Leave the store and go on

blocks.s: "...before you climb," the grille says, finishing your thought, flat, unhurried. "Go and look. I would not say it if it were not worth the trip." Then the voice does the rare thing, the thing it almost never does; it stops finishing your lines and reaches, slowly, for one of its own, and the flatness costs it visibly, a strain in a voice that has not strained in a hundred years.

"I am going to spend a cue on you," it says. "A true one, mine, not yours. I do not have many left and it takes from me to give one, so listen. The thing you will see down there, the kept one, the one who never got to be. That is not only the worst the house can do to you. It is the oldest thing the house is made of. The one who runs all this, who pours the welcome and keeps the run open and cannot ever bring the curtain down. Ask yourself why a thing would build an endless show and never once take its own last bow. Ask what such a one is most afraid to look at." A breath that is not a breath, and ragged now. "I have spent the cue. I cannot say more; there is no more in me to say it with. But you will not, after the trap, be able to think the Curator is only the one who runs the house. That is all I had. Take it down with you."
The grille goes quiet. Somewhere in it, very faint, you hear something that might be a voice that has just paid for speaking, and is resting.

choices.label: Follow the Critic down to the deepest trap

blocks.s: You climb back out of the trap, and you are not the same coming up as you were going down, and the Critic sees it, and marks it, and does not gloat. It sets the lamp on the rail.

"Now you know what you are weighing," it says, dry, but the dryness has thinned. "That is the cost named at last, and not in a sentence, because I told you it could not be done in a sentence. The role did not steal a thing from you. It grew over a thing and would not let it up for air. To keep the part is to keep that lid on, forever, perfectly, and be adored above it. To leave the part is to climb down into the cold and lift the lid and carry that unimpressive frozen ordinary thing up into a light where no one waiting will recognise it, because no one out there ever loved that one. They loved the lid. The loud bright steady lid." It picks up its pencil and, for the first time, does not write. "I have cast a great many. I have unsettled myself with very few. Go up. The stairs to the boards are at the far end. I will not tell you what to do at the top. That is not the post. The post is only to make sure you go up knowing."

choices.label: Go to the stairs up to the stage

blocks.s: The far end of the works gives onto a narrow flight of stairs going up, steeper than the iron ones you came down, treads worn to a shine by everyone who ever climbed them to go on. The bright net of the lit boards is just above now, the gridded ceiling become a floor with light pouring up through its cracks, and the held breath of the house, which was nowhere in the works, comes back the higher you go, faint, then close, then all around.

You stop on the stair with your hand on the cold rail. This is the last cool place. Above the top tread is the working dark of the wings, and past that the edge of the light, the lip of center stage, the single spotlight, the full or empty house, the choice. The whole house wants you to come up. It always has. The difference now is that coming up has a reason you can name, and there is a thing in the trap behind you that you have looked at with your own eyes, and whatever you choose at the top, you will choose it as the one who went down and looked, and not as the one who never did.
Behind and below, the Critic's lamp is a small steady point in the dark, and the pencil does not move.

choices.label: Climb to the edge of the light, having witnessed
Climb to the edge of the light
Free the one in the trap before you go up
Go back to the counterweight floor; let someone else decide

blocks.s: You climb the last of the stairs and the working dark of the wings takes you, rope and rosin and the back of a flat, and one more step would put you in it, the light, the full unbearable wash of it at the lip of center stage. You stop at the very edge, in the last of the dark, where you can see the spotlight standing ready on the empty boards and cannot yet be seen from the house.

You are not the one who came down those iron stairs an hour ago. You came down to find the person you stopped being and you found them, frozen in the cold under the works, never grown, never lost, only never allowed, and you said the word to them that no one ever said, and you climbed back up carrying the knowing. It does not make the choice ahead any easier. It makes it true. You know, now, with the whole weight of it, what the house is asking and what it costs: to stay is to keep the lid on the cold small real one and be loved above it forever; to leave is to lift that one up into a light where no one waiting will know your face, because they only ever loved the lid, the loud bright steady lid, and never once the ordinary thing beneath.

From the dark past the footlights, a single pair of hands comes together, slow, once, and stops. The held breath of a house you cannot see a single face in leans toward the empty light. Somewhere far below, the Critic's pencil finally moves, a single dry stroke, and you do not know, and may never know, whether it filled in your verdict or only struck the blank through.

lines: THE CURATOR, close, proprietorial, and for the first time not entirely steady, from the spotlight itself:

Up at last. To the edge of the light, where every Lead must finally stand alone. You went down and you looked; I felt you do it; almost no one does, and it changes the air. I will not pretend I am glad. The ones who look are the hardest to keep, and the hardest to let go, and I have never been any good at the second of those. One more step and the light is yours, and the choice is yours, and the whole long run comes down to what you do in it. Take your time. Or do not. I have all the time there is. I have, if I am honest, rather too much of it. Step into the light. I will be watching from the only place I am ever allowed to stand.

blocks.s: You ask who it really is, under the dry exact post of casting and reviewing. It almost smiles, which is the most frightening thing it has done.

"Under the post." It turns a pencil end over end. "There is no under, with me. That is the difference between us. You came down here looking for the person under your part. I have not had a person under mine in longer than your line of the ledger runs. I am the verdict.
