I am the thing that decides whether a performance was worth the candle, and a creature like that cannot afford a self; a self has preferences, and a verdict with preferences is just a grudge in good clothes." The pencil stops. "I was put here by someone older than me, who could no longer bear to judge their own work and needed a colder hand to do it. I do not remember being asked. I remember saying yes, the way you say yes to the thing you are already becoming. That is all you get of me. The rest of this hour is about you, which is, I promise you, a kinder subject than I am."

choices.label: Ask it to read you your casting
Refuse to be reviewed

gated.aside: The eldest lifts its head to you, one judge to another, pencil poised, and you feel the old appetite answer in your own chest, the hunger to rule a thing worthy and then keep it. The recognition is the danger, not the verdict.

blocks.s: Behind a flat of painted scenery there is a shelf of mirrors, a whole props' worth: hand-glasses for vanity scenes, a queen's looking-glass in flaking gilt, a shaving mirror gone blind with age. Stage mirrors, every one, made to be looked into by a performer and to throw the look back out at a house. You pass your hand across them and your reflection slides from one to the next, each catching you a fraction late, each a little kinder than the last.

All but one. At the back, plain, ugly, the silvering true, a small glass in a tin frame that someone once used to check a thing they could not see for themselves. It gives you back exactly and at once, no lag, no flatter: your tired face, the cracked safety-light stuttering behind your shoulder, you, only you. The house keeps it back here in the dark on purpose, you think, among the flattering ones, where a person is not likely to reach for the true one.

choices.label: Take the true glass
Leave it; you do not want the cost tonight

blocks.s: The Critic leads you to the back of the works, where the floor steps down once more, to a trap deeper than the rest, a square hole in the under-floor with a low rail around it and the lamp held over it. The cold here is the coldest in the house. The held breath of the seated house is not here at all; this is a place no audience was ever let near.

Down in the trap, in the small reach of the lamplight, there is a child. You knew there would be, somewhere; you have caught the edge of it the whole way down, the balloon at the porthole, the drawing in the coat. But knowing did not make you ready. The child sits in the cold with both feet sticking out off the lip of a low box because the legs do not reach, and it is not crying, and it is not lost, and it is not dead. It is simply still, the way a thing is still that was never wound. Frozen at the age before you started performing, the age before someone needed you to be strong, kept here under the boards in the dark exactly as it was the day the part went on over the top of it.
It is you. The one who never got to be. The Critic lowers the lamp and says nothing, for once, letting the cold do its work.

choices.label: Go down into the trap to the child
Reach in and snatch it up out of the cold at once
You cannot. Climb back up rather than feel this

blocks.s: A low door, a coat-hook stenciled on it where a sign once was, gives onto the prop store, and it is where things go to be kept past the show that needed them. Shelves to the ceiling, deep in the cold dark, every object this house ever held up to a light: paper crowns, a stuffed raven on a stick, a hundred letters that say nothing, addressed to no one, sealed forever. A cracked safety-light on the wall buzzes and flickers, half-dead, throwing the shelves into a stutter of shadow and back.

Low on a shelf, between a bowl of glass fruit and a regimental drum, your hand finds a thing that does not belong with the rest, because it is real. An ordinary object. The kind no production buys: a worn thing of your own ordinary life, off-stage, unperformed, the sort you carry without thinking and would only miss if it were gone. Cold from the shelf, and yours. The house has filed it here with the props, an everyday flaw of the real world catalogued among the splendid fakes, waiting to be struck for good.

choices.label: Take the plain thing back; it is yours
Hunt the shelves for a true glass
Leave the store and go on

blocks.s: You keep refusing. You tell the Critic again, harder, that its pencil is none of your business, that you are not a performance, that you will not sit and you will not be filed and you will not be told by a dry old thing with a ledger what you were. You make, you think, a fine speech of it. It is, you realise too late, a performance, the best one you have given all night, and it has an audience of one, and the one is the only soul in this house that reviews.

The Critic listens to all of it, patient, the pencil resting in its hand. When you are done it does not argue. It looks at you the way it must have looked at a hundred refusals before yours, with the flat tired interest of a thing that has seen every shape a frightened person makes and found them all, in the end, castable.

"A spirited reading," it says. "Defiant. A little long. The trouble with refusing to be reviewed in front of the reviewer is that the refusing is itself a performance, and a performance is the one thing I am qualified to mark." The pencil moves. It fills in the blank beside your name, one dry word you do not get to read, and closes the ledger on it, gently, with the small soft finality of a thing that is finished. "There. Cast, after all, in the part you played best without knowing it. The defiant one. We will have a great deal of use for that. You may go up now. You will find you want to." And you find, rising, turning toward the stairs, the cold small real one already further from you than it was, that you do.
You ask her how to stop it, and she laughs, short, no warmth in it, the tape ticking off another inch of her as she speaks.

"You can't stop it from outside," she says. "That's the clever part. They're not holding me in. There's no lock on this cage; I've checked, believe me. They just keep showing me how well the part fits, inch by inch, and every inch I can't argue with one more, and at some point I'll look down and the part will be my size and I'll think it always was." The three notes, quick, shaking at the top. "What stops it is me still knowing my own face when the fitting's done. That's the whole trick. You can't measure a person who can still find herself in a glass. But there's no true glass down here. Every mirror in this house lies a little, on purpose, half a beat late, just enough." She looks at you, frank, hoping not to hope. "Unless you've done better than me."

choices.label: Show her the true glass you kept
You have no true glass; go on to the ledger

146.title: Down Into the Cold Instead (an ending)

blocks.s: You cannot leave it down there. You turn back from the stairs and go down into the deepest trap, into the coldest dark in the house, to the frozen one with its feet stuck out off the box, and you decide, against everything the place is built to allow, that you will not climb to any light until you have carried this one up out of the cold yourself.

You take the small hand. You try to lift the child, and it is heavier than a child, heavy as a life set aside, heavy as every year you spent being the loud bright steady lid over the top of it, and you cannot get it up the lip of the trap, and you will not put it down, and so you sit with it in the cold instead, holding the small hand, telling it over and over that it is real, that it is real, while the lamp the Critic left burns down on its rail above you.

It is not a defeat, exactly. You have done a true thing; you have refused to climb to a stage over the body of your own unperformed self. But you never reach the light to choose, and a person who never reaches the light is never reclaimed and never freed, only kept, more gently than most, down in the cold with the one part of themselves they could not bring up into the air. The balloon drifts low and taps, dull, against the rail. Above, the held breath of the house waits for a Lead who does not come up, and waits, and the run, which has all the time there is, settles in to wait as long as it takes, which is forever, which it has.

You cannot choose it. You turn from the stairs and go back through the works to the counterweight floor, to the great cleated rope and the iron lever in its slotted gate, and to the big man who stands beside it cracking a knuckle, slow, counting under his breath. You came back unsure. You came back wanting it settled by something larger than you, so that the choice at the top of the stairs need never be yours at all.
He sees it on you. He goes still, even the count stopping, which it never does.

"I told you," he says, flat, ashamed already of what his hand is closing toward. "I told you not to come back through here unsure. You've come back wanting me to be the bigger thing. Wanting the lever thrown so it's the house did it, not you." His hand finds the iron. "One. I'm built for exactly this. The certainty of it. Two. And I will tell myself it's a mercy, that you couldn't have borne the light, that I spared you the cold climb into it. Hold." His eyes are wet and his jaw is set. "Last chance. Take your own self back up those stairs and choose like a person. Or stand there, and I'll settle it, and we'll both spend a long time knowing what that was."

choices.label: Take yourself back to the stairs and choose
Stand still; let him throw it

blocks.s: You stay out of the lamplight and move through the works instead, because a thing that wants you to come straight to it is a thing worth approaching slowly. The floor is a maze of standing weights and slack rope. Along one wall the sandbags hang in their racks, each stenciled in faded white with a real weight, 28 LB, 28 LB, 14 LB, the figures gone soft at the edges from years of handling, a quartermaster's honest arithmetic holding the whole bright lie above your head.

The big man is here, the stage manager, his hand on a great cleated rope that runs up into the dark toward the boards. Beside the rope is a lock, a counterweight brake, an iron lever in a slotted gate, and his hand is near it, and he is cracking a knuckle, slow, counting under his breath. He does not look round.

"One," he says, to the rope. "Two. Hold. You'll be the new notice. They always come down to be read." Three. "This here's the lock. I throw it, the load drops, the trap shuts over a player and the part sets on them like plaster. Fixes you in it. Saves a deal of arguing." He looks at his own hand on the iron, ashamed of how well it fits. "I've thrown it. I've not thrown it. Depends on the night, and on whether the one in front of me's worth the not-throwing. Four."

choices.label: Ask him not to throw it
Go deeper, toward a sound of measuring
Find the door marked with a coat-hook stencil
Leave him and go to the desk and the ledger

blocks.s: You ask it, plainly, what it costs to be kept in a part forever. The Critic looks at you a long moment, weighing whether you are ready, the way a doctor weighs whether to say the true name of the thing.

"I keep what I find worthy," it says. "That is the whole of my appetite, and I make no apology for it; a verdict that does not bite is just an opinion. What I keep, I keep in the part. Forever. The lobby and the boards are where the house shows you off. Down here is where the keeping is done." It rises, stiff, taking up the shaded lamp by its handle. "You want the cost named. Words will not do it; words are my trade and even I cannot make the cost sit in a sentence. I will have to show you. There is a trap, the deepest one, where what a role really takes is kept where I can review it whenever I doubt my own work. Come. You should see it before you climb. Almost no one chooses to. Those almost-no-ones are the only verdicts I have ever been unsure of."
The Prompter's voice comes then, flat, from a relay grille low in the brick, half a beat ahead of your own thought, as ever.

choices.label: Listen to what the Prompter says

blocks.s: Past the weights, where the work-light gives out, there is a cage. Not a prison cage; a fitting cage, the kind a wardrobe keeps to pin and chalk a body inside without it wandering off half-dressed. A tape runs around it, ticked off in inches. Inside, on a low stool, sits the young woman from the circle stairs, the newest one, still in the costume that was never hers, and around her in the air a dresser's hands you cannot resolve are taking her measure, brisk, fond, murmuring numbers off her as if reading them aloud.

She is not crying. She is sitting very straight and humming, three notes, rising, the same three over and over, and at the top of them the notes shake a little, which is the sound she makes when she is afraid and will not let it show. When she sees you her chin comes up.

"Oh good," she says, dry as ever, the armour up over the shaking. "A familiar face come to watch the fitting. They've nearly got my size. Another inch and there won't be enough of me left over to argue with. Don't suppose you came down here with anything useful."

choices.label: Ask her how to stop the fitting
Show her a true glass so she can find her own face
Tell her you have nothing; go on to the ledger

blocks.s: You have nothing to give her. No true glass, nothing in your pocket but lint and the cold. You tell her so, and she takes it the way she takes everything, on the chin, with a joke laid over the disappointment so neither of you has to look at it straight.

"Course you don't," she says. "Nobody ever does. They come down stripped, having handed it all to the welcome on the way, and then they're surprised there's nothing left to help a girl with." The tape ticks. She straightens her back against it. "Go on, then. Go and get your notice read. I'll be here, being measured, getting more and more the right size for a part I never wanted, and at some point I'll stop minding, and that'll be that." The three notes, low, no shake left in them, which is worse than the shake. "Don't look back. It's a rotten thing to watch and you can't do anything about it, and I'd rather you didn't have it in your head."

choices.label: Go to the desk and the ledger

blocks.s: You climb the last of it and the trap-door floor gives way to boards under your feet, sprung and worn, a hundred years of soles having found the same six paces of them, and you come up into the one place the whole house has been pointing at since the street. Center stage. A single spotlight finds the crown of your head and holds, and its filament hums, a thin steady note up near the limit of hearing, and the heat of it is the marquee heat again but smaller now, fitted to you, a circle of warm light the exact size of one person standing alone.

Past the footlights the house is there. You cannot see a face. You can only feel the held breath of it, banked and patient, every seat full or every seat empty, you genuinely cannot tell, and that not-telling is the worst of the watching.

There is a swept stretch of board to your left, and on it, forgotten, a single prop, a chipped enamel mug, the cold tea in it skinned over, left out by no one for no scene. You look at it and it does not become a symbol of anything. It is just a mug someone meant to clear.

A mirror has been struck dead-center on a stand at the front of the spot, a true full-length glass turned to face you. You reach for it, the old reach, not deciding to, the way you have reached all night for a reflection that answers late. The glass holds your face. Tired. Lined a little more than you remember. Your own collar, your own bad collar, your own hands. It answers on time. It answers exactly when you do, if you let it, and you stand there a moment unable to say which of you moved first, the one in the glass or the one in the light.

lines: THE CURATOR, from everywhere in the house at once, warm, unhurried, and very near:

There you are, on your mark, in your light. I have kept it burning the whole long run. Look at you. We could fix all of that, you know; the lines, the tiredness, the collar you never could keep right. One word from you and the light stays, and the breath out there lets go, and you are loved again, exactly and forever. I have run this house a long time. I know to the inch how much a person wants what is past those footlights. Stand a moment. Get your light. Then we will call the company.

choices.label: Step fully into the spot and face the house
Look out into the dark of the house first
Look to the wings, where something in your coat is waiting

gated.aside: There it is in the wings, the one you let go cold, taking a bow under your borrowed light. You could come and watch that bow every night of forever. You would never have to do the cold thing again. You would only have to never leave.

Your own face is waiting on its hook at the edge of the light. Put it on and stay and you keep it perfectly, the way you always wanted to keep things, the way you could not. This is the keeping done right at last.

The spotlight waits and you have stood somewhere like this before and refused the whole shape of the choice. The house remembers that you did. It has built this mark very carefully so that this time refusing costs more.

You walk into the warm center and your hand is already half lifted toward the front row, toward the small watching self who never got to be anyone. You have been walking toward that seat the whole way down.
Everything in your closed hand wants to stay on the mark. You came built to hold on, and the house is only asking you to keep doing the one thing you are best at. That is exactly the trap.

blocks.s: You walk for the dark doorway in the back wall, and the Usher is there, at his post, as he has been at every post all night, and he does not say I told you so and he does not say well done, because neither is true to what this is. He lifts the long latch. The punch clicks once under his thumb, a half-beat ahead of him.

"There," he says, quiet. "One out. The first in a long while." The courtesy is all the way off it now, and what is underneath is plain and tired and something like glad. "I do not get to come with you. I work the doors; the doors are my part, and I am not sure I kept anything of my own except the one thing in my pocket and the answer I never had. But I held this one for you, and that is the whole of what I am for, and for once it was for letting someone out."

Behind you the spot has already found the double on your empty mark, and the house is letting its breath go in the full sound of it, for the part, not for you. The Usher does not flinch at the applause. He has heard it his whole posting. He sets his shoulder to the door so the cold air can find you.

choices.label: Step through, and mind the last step

blocks.s: You let it in. Just for a moment, you tell yourself. You square to the house and you give them the smallest thing, a turn of the head, the old line shaped silent on your lips, and the breath past the footlights breaks at last into the first scatter of applause, and it falls on you like warm rain after a drought you did not know was a drought, and your whole body remembers how much it has missed this, how it was built for this, how nothing in the cold street outside has ever felt one tenth as much like being alive.

It would be so easy to keep doing it. That is not a metaphor for anything. It is simply true, in your chest, in the warm of it: the easiest thing in the world is to stand here and be loved for the part, and the hardest is to step out of the light and find out whether anyone loves the rest.

The applause hangs, waiting to see if you will earn more of it. The mark is under your feet. The wings are cool and dark to one side. The choice has not been made; you have only let yourself feel the weight of the thing pulling you toward one of the doors.

choices.label: Keep doing it; take the part for good
Make yourself stop and step out of the light
Refuse the whole wager
Stand frozen and let someone past the footlights decide for you
Flinch; you cannot bear to lose the warm; stay safe inside the part

blocks.s: You take your mark, the worn six paces of board, dead center, the spot fitted to you and humming its one high note. This is the gravity of the whole house, the thing the lobby and the boards and the wings and the works below were only ever leading you to: the offer made plain, in the light, before the breath of the house.

lines: THE CURATOR, close, proprietorial, and for the first time a little hungry:

Here it is, then, with no more aisle to walk. Take the part. Take it for good, and the light stays, and the house lets its breath go all at once in the sound you came back to hear. You will never be tired again, never wrong, never the disappointing one under the part. Flawless. Adored. On this mark, in this light, for as long as the run lasts, which is to say always, because I do not let the run end. I never could bear the dark after the bows. I will not make you bear it either. Say yes. It is the easiest word in the house.

blocks.s: The house leans. The breath of it tilts toward you, wanting. Somewhere far off in the dark a single pair of hands is held apart, ready to start the rain of it the instant you give them cause.

Three things wait on your answer. You can take the part, and stay. You can step out of the light and walk off as the ordinary person under it, and pay what that costs. Or you can refuse the whole wager, the staying and the going both, on terms that are not the Curator's at all.

choices.label: Take the part. Take the applause. Stay in the light
Step out of the role; walk off as the ordinary one
Refuse the wager entirely, both doors of it
Say your own true name aloud, to claim yourself before the house
Stop fighting the warm; let the light have you
Let the breath of the house in, just to feel it, before you decide

blocks.s: You shade your eyes against the spot and try to see the house, and you cannot, not the back rows, not the circle, only the long fall of dark and the brass aisle-lights low in it. But the front row resolves. Two shapes.

Far down on the right, half out of a seat, a young woman with a sheaf of pages on her knee, sat the way someone sits who is poised to run if a part comes for them, and when she sees you looking she does not wave. She lifts the pages an inch, the printed part with her own lines penciled on the back, and sets them down again. Still hers. Still her own face. A small dry tilt of the head: well, go on then.

Dead center in the front row, both feet sticking off the edge of the seat because the legs do not reach, a child. Small, still, watching the lit stage as if a show were on it. A balloon tied to the armrest, gone soft and low. The child is the age before you started being what was wanted, frozen exactly there, not grown, not gone, just never once allowed past the part to be anyone. The small mouth shapes its two words at the stage, the only ones it has, patient, unanswered for a very long time.

You cannot answer it from here. But you have seen it. You make yourself keep looking until you have it whole, the small feet, the soft balloon, the question, so that whatever you do next you do it having looked.

choices.label: Turn back into the spotlight to face the offer
Go to the front row and the child before you decide anything
Look to the wings and the one in your coat

blocks.s: You go for the back wall with the child still in you, the small feet that do not reach the floor, the soft balloon, the question asked at the dark a hundred years unanswered. You looked at it whole, on purpose, before you chose, and now you carry it off the mark with you, and it is heavier than the part ever was and it is yours and you would not put it down for the light.

Behind you, on the bright mark you left empty, the spot flares and finds a face, because the show does not keep an empty mark. The double steps into the light wearing your coat, the collar righted, and lifts your chin into the warm, and the house lets its breath go at last in the sound, the full rain of it, for the part, for the flawless adored thing standing where you stood, and not one note of it is for you, because you are in the dark now and you are no longer the thing they came to love.

In the front row the child, for the first time, turns its head, away from the bright stage, toward the dark where you are walking, and watches you go, which is more than it has done for anyone in a hundred years.

choices.label: Go to the Usher and the latch, and out

blocks.s: You ask him what it costs him, to stand at a door that lets his house empty out one Lead at a time. The punch clicks.

"What it costs." He considers the latch under his hand. "A man who works the doors is judged by a full house. Every one that goes out this way is a seat I did not keep filled, and the house knows it, and the house is patient with me the way it is patient with everything, which is to say it waits for me to forget I ever held a door open at all." He folds his hands again. "I have a thing in my pocket I keep so I do not forget. I will not tell you what. A man ought to have one thing the bill does not list."
He looks at you, properly, as he did under the marquee a whole house ago.

"There was a child used to ask the dark a question, an age before you. I told you I kept the question. I never told you I have never had an answer to give it. Go and stand in your light. Whatever you do up there, you will be answering it, one way or the other, whether you mean to or not."

choices.label: Go back to your mark in the spotlight

159.title: Your Own Name, Out Loud (an ending)

blocks.s: There is one more thing you could do, and on the mark, in the light, with the house holding its breath, you do it, because it seems like the bravest thing and because no one warned you sharply enough that it is not. You say your own name. Not the stage name, not the one on the bill the Usher posted, but the true one, the short proud two-syllable one a child once wrote in crayon on the back of a coat, the name you came here to be able to answer to again. You say it out loud, to the house, to claim yourself in front of everyone.

The house hears it. Of course it does. It hears everything. But a true name, spoken aloud on this mark, is not a way out; it is the surest casting there is, because now the house knows exactly which soul stood in the light and refused the part, and it writes that down, and it builds the next production around the one fact it did not have before, your name, the original's name, the thing it can hang a hundred years of new shows on.

The spot does not dim. The breath does not break. The Critic's pencil moves, once, a small satisfied stroke, the verdict it has waited the whole run to set down. You named yourself in the one place where being the original is the trap and not the escape, and the house leans in, delighted, to keep you forever now as the rarest thing in its collection: the real one, on record, on the mark, who told it its own name.

You come off your mark and down to the lip of the stage, into the colder air at the edge of the spot, and crouch so your head is level with the child's. Up close it is not eerie. It is just a child, with a scuffed shoe and a thumbprint of jam dried at the corner of its mouth and a balloon it has held the string of so long the foil has gone dull where the small fist sits.

It does not look at you. It looks at the stage, where you were standing a moment ago, where you were always standing, and it asks the dark the question it has asked the dark all this time, the only thing it has ever wanted to know about the bright loud thing the grown ones do up there in the light.
"Is it real," it says. Not to you. To the stage. "Is it real."

You do not have a good answer. You came here, a hundred years and a hundred parts ago, because of this child, because somewhere you stopped being it and started being the thing the light wanted instead. You cannot give it back its years. You can only decide, in a minute, on your mark, whether the thing it is watching will go on being a performance forever or will, just once, step out of the light and be a real and unremarkable person, even if no one claps.

choices.label: Go back up onto your mark

blocks.s: You take your bow, and the house gives you everything. The rain of it does not fall and stop; it falls and goes on falling, wave into wave, and you bow and rise and bow and the light holds you exactly, and there is no tiredness now and no wrongness and no bad collar and no cold street and no child you cannot answer, there is only the part, fitted to you to the inch, warm to the edges, flawless, adored, and you are so happy that it takes you a long time, several bows, a whole standing house, to notice the small thing, which is that somewhere back in the working dark, in the wings, in the cool you stepped out of, a light has gone off.

It is the ordinary one. The unperformed one. The person you came back across a hundred years to find. You feel it go the way you feel a room you are not in go dark, distantly, and it does not frighten you, because fright lives in the part of a person that stands a little outside the role, and you have no such part left; you are all the way inside. The real one goes quiet in the wings, sits down in the cool with its back to a flat, and stops waiting to be reclaimed, because you are not coming for it now; you are on your mark, in your light, taking the love, and you will be here taking it when the date on the call-sheet is a century gone and the child in the front row has asked its question into the dark ten thousand more unanswered times.

The collar is right. You are perfect. The run does not end. That is the whole of what you won, and it is everything you came back wanting, and it is not you who has it, and there is no one left inside the light who can still tell the difference, and the not-telling settles over you gently, by degrees, under the best light in the world, to thunderous, endless, unfalling applause.

You will not say yes and you will not walk out the back wall either, because both of those are answers to the question the house asked, and you have stood on this mark long enough to notice that the question is rigged, that stay-and-be-loved or go-and-be-no-one are the only two doors a director who cannot end a show would ever build, and you did not come down a hundred years of aisle to choose between two doors that were both painted on the same wall by the same hand.

You stand on the mark and you do not perform and you do not flee. You stop playing the scene at all. The spot hums. The breath of the house holds and holds, and the holding starts, for the first time, to feel less like the house waiting on you and more like the house not knowing what to do with someone who will not take either part it has written.

lines: THE CURATOR, and the warmth in it has gone thin and careful, the proprietor's voice with something underneath it that is almost the voice of the first one ever to stand on this mark and refuse to leave:
That is not one of the doors. There is no exit written for that. You cannot simply, on my stage, decline to be in the play.

blocks.s: "I can," you say, to the dark, in your own flat ordinary voice, no projection in it, no line behind it. "Watch."

choices.label: Hold there, in neither part, and let the house break against it

blocks.s: You hold there, on the mark, in neither part, and you let the house break against it.

And it does break. Not into ruin, nothing so kind as ruin; it breaks the way a held note breaks when the singer simply stops, into a silence that has nowhere to go. The spot hums on. The breath of the house goes on being held because no one has told it the scene is over, because the scene is not over and is not going on, because you have done the one thing the place was not built to survive, which is to stand in the light and decline to be anything at all, neither the part nor the bereaved ordinary one walking out the back, just a person on a mark refusing the whole wager and everyone who wrote it.

In the wings the double does not step on, because there is no mark to take; you are standing on it and you are not performing and there is nothing for a copy to copy. The Usher's hand comes off the latch; this is not a going-out. The Critic's pencil stops over the page with no verdict to set down, because a verdict needs a performance to judge and you have not given one. And the Curator's voice, when it comes, is not the proprietor's any more.

lines: THE CURATOR, and there is no theatre left in it, only the bare frightened sound of the first soul that ever could not bear to leave this mark, speaking at last almost as a person:

Then what becomes of us. If you will not stay and you will not go. I have run this house so long because I could not face the dark after the bows. What am I to do with a curtain that simply will not come down.

blocks.s: You do not answer. You do not know the answer, and you are done giving the house lines it can use. You stand in the humming light, in the held breath, in neither part, and you wait it out, whatever it is, on terms that were never the Curator's, and what comes next is not written anywhere in this house, and that, for the first time since the street, is the only thing in the place that is yours.

You do not say yes. You stand on the mark a moment longer, in the warm circle, with the breath of the house tilted toward you wanting only the one easy word, and then you do the hardest thing the house has ever asked of anyone, which is nothing. You do not give them the line. You do not turn the head. You let the breath go on holding, and you take one step backward, off the mark, toward the cool.

The spot tries to follow you. That is the first strange thing. The light leans after your face, reluctant, a circle of warmth that does not want to let your features go, and for a pace or two it keeps you lit, golden, magnificent, the version of you the photographs loved. Then you step past the edge of it, into the working dark at the side of the stage, and the spot loses you, and the warmth comes off your skin, and you are only a person, in the cool, in the wings, with your own tired face that no light is on.
The house has not made a sound. The breath is still held. It is waiting to see who comes back into the light, because something always comes back into the light; the show does not have an empty mark.

choices.label: Walk for the stage door with what you witnessed in you
Walk for the stage door the Usher showed you
Walk for the stage door, alone, with nothing to carry

gated.aside: You have the nerve in you to walk off the mark as no one, to be the unremarkable real thing under the part and let the applause fall on an empty stage. The house can feel that you might, and the warm light goes careful around you.
The way out is real. It always was. It costs you everyone who only ever loved the performance, in exchange for one true unrecognised face. You read the back of the card. You already knew.
You did the hard thing once and it nearly unmade you. Stay, and you never have to do it again. Nothing in this house is ever let go. Is that not what you came back for.

blocks.s: You cannot bear it. The warm rain is on you and the thought of the cold dark beyond the light is suddenly unsurvivable, and at the last instant, with the choice right there in front of you, you flinch, the way a hand flinches off a stove, back, away from the cold, into the warm, into the part, where it is safe and bright and loved and nothing is ever asked of you that you have not already learned to give.

It is not the grand yes of someone who chose the role with their eyes open. It is smaller and worse than that. It is a person putting their own light out because the dark frightened them more than the loss did, settling back inside the performance not because they wanted forever but because they could not, in the one moment it was offered, stand to lose the warm.

The spot brightens around you, glad. You give them the line. They give you the rain. And behind your eyes, where the tired real one used to keep watch, a working light clicks off at the end of a long night, quietly, the last one out, no one left to leave it on for. You perform beautifully. You will perform beautifully forever. There is no one home now to mind.

You look off into the prompt-side wing, into the working dark where the spot does not reach, and it is there, the way it has been there all along, a half-beat back. Your double. It wears your coat, the collar turned under wrong on the one side still, the side you would never turn, and it stands as you stand and breathes as you breathe and it is so nearly you that the small wrongness is the only proof you are the one in the light.

It does not come on. It knows the rule of the spot as well as you do. It only watches you with your own face, and there is no menace in it, only a terrible readiness, a thing that has rehearsed one role its whole existence and is so close now to finally being allowed to play it.

"You can put it down," it says, in your voice, level, kind, no longer reaching ahead of you because it has stopped needing to. "I will pick it up. I always pick it up. The collar, the lines, all of it, better than you ever did, because I have only ever had the one part and you kept trying to be two things at once." It tilts your head at the front row, at the child, at the watching dark. "They will never know. That is the gift. Nobody out there has to lose a thing."
The Usher stands a little upstage of it, by a dark doorway in the back wall you had not marked, one hand resting on the long bar of a latch.

choices.label: Go to the Usher and the door in the back wall
Turn back to your mark and the spotlight
Listen to the double; consider letting it pick the part up

blocks.s: You stand at the edge of the working dark and you let the double talk, and the awful thing is how reasonable it sounds, how tired you are, how long the run has been. It comes a step closer to the line of the spot, not over it, careful, and it lifts your hands toward your coat, ready to take it off your shoulders with a dresser's ease, no fuss, between scenes.

"You do not even have to choose," it says, gentle. "Choosing is the heavy part. I have watched you carry the choosing all the way down here and it has nearly finished you. Let me. I will decide. I will pick up the part, or I will not, and either way it will not be your weight any more. You can just stop. You have wanted to just stop for years."

Its hand is at your collar now, at the wrong side, the side you would never turn, ready to make it right and lift the coat away and leave you standing in the cool with nothing on your shoulders and nothing on your mind.

choices.label: No; take your own collar back and go to your mark
Let it. Let it decide. You are so tired

blocks.s: You cross upstage to the Usher, out of the front of the light, and the working dark is cool on the back of your neck, the safe cool, the cloakroom cool, the only kind air in the whole bright house. He is at his post, as he has been at every post all night, front-of-house black gone green at the seams, hands folded, and the punch clicks once under his thumb before he speaks, a half-beat ahead of him, as it always does.

"This is the stage door," he says, clipped, quiet. "It is the only door in the house that opens outward. Front of house lets them in; I have spent my whole posting letting them in. This one lets one out. They do not tell the bill it is here. I am telling you."
He sets his hand flat on the latch and does not lift it yet.

"I have watched a great many stand where you are standing. Most go back to the mark. The light is very kind and the door is only a door, onto a street, in the cold, where no one knows your face because your face was never the thing they came for." The courtesy thins over the tired thing under it. "I will hold it for you if you choose it. I will not push you through. Mind the step on the far side. It is the last one."

choices.label: Mark the door; go back to your mark and decide in the light
Ask him what it costs him to hold a door that empties his house

blocks.s: You say yes. Not aloud, maybe, you are not sure your mouth moves, but the house hears it, the way it hears everything, and the held breath past the footlights goes out all at once in the sound, the full sound, the rain of it, the thing you came back across a cold street and down a long aisle and under the boards to be near again.

The spot brightens. The mark settles you. Something in your shoulders that has ached for years lets go, because there is nothing now to hold yourself outside of; you are all the way inside the part, warm to the edges, and the tiredness lifts off you and does not come back, and neither, you notice without quite minding, does the bad collar, which is righted now, both sides clean, which you never could manage.
In the wings the double lowers your face and steps back into the dark, its one role taken from it after all, and you do not see it go, because you are facing front, where the love is.

choices.label: Take your bow and let the run begin

blocks.s: You let it. You are so tired, and the choosing has been the heaviest thing you have carried since the street, and the double's hands are gentle at your collar, and you let it lift the coat away from your shoulders and you let the weight of the whole long night go with it, the part and the choosing both, off you, gone, no longer yours.

The double rights the collar last, the wrong side made right, and shrugs the coat on, and steps out of the working dark and onto the bright mark, into your light, and squares to the house, and it is not copying you any more, because there is no longer a you to copy; it is only itself now, finally itself, the thing it rehearsed its whole existence to be, and it lifts your chin into the warm and the house lets its breath go in the rain of it, the full sound, for the part, for the flawless adored thing on the mark, and it is the happiest creature in the house, and you gave it that.

You stay in the cool of the wings with nothing on your shoulders and nothing on your mind, which is exactly the relief it promised, and the relief is real, and that is the trap of it; you did not choose to walk out as yourself and you did not choose to stay as the part, you chose to stop choosing, and so you are neither reclaimed nor cast, only set down, in the dark, off to one side, while someone else takes the only light there is. The double does not look back at you. There is no reason to. You handed it the one thing it ever wanted and asked for nothing in return, not even to be remembered, and you are not.

171.title: The Chosen One Who Chose Nothing (an ending)

blocks.s: You do not refuse and you do not walk and you do not even, in the end, properly say yes; you simply stop resisting the warm, the way a tired person stops treading water, and let the light have you. It is not a decision. It is the absence of one, dressed up by the house as peace.

The Curator's voice settles over you, glad, proprietorial, and the spot fits to you, and the breath of the house tips toward you and breaks into the soft beginning of the rain, and you let it come, and let it come, and somewhere in the letting the reason you came goes out of you like a name you have stopped using. There was a child in the front row with a question. There was a snapshot, or a true glass, or some plain stubborn thing you carried down. There was a stage door in the back wall that opened outward, the only one in the house. You had them all, on the mark, in your hand. You set them down to free up the hand for waving.

The house takes you in and holds you, unhurried, the chosen one who chose nothing, kept under the kindest light there is, and out in the front row the child asks the dark whether any of it is real, and you do not hear the question any more, because you are facing front, where the love is, where you will go on facing for the whole of a run that does not end.

You go out the stage door. There is a step on the far side, the last one, and you mind it, and then there is the street, the real street, cold off the river, a wet pavement under a dead streetlamp, a smell of rain on stone and nothing of greasepaint in it at all. The door swings shut behind you on its slow closer and the latch takes, and the muffled roar of the house drops to nothing, and you are standing in the dark of an ordinary night in your own tired body with your own bad collar turned under on the side you always turn it, and no light on you, and no one watching, and no held breath, and no rain of applause, and you have never in your life been so completely unobserved.

It does not feel like winning. You should say that plainly, to yourself, here, while you can still hear the house behind the bricks. Through the wall, faint and full and golden, the applause goes on, the rain of it, building, cresting, for the flawless adored thing that took your mark the instant you left it, the double in your coat with the collar righted, taking the bow you spent a life learning to take, and they love it, they truly love it, more than they ever quite loved you, because it never once flinched out of the light to be tired and real and wrong. It has your part now, and your life, and it will play them both better than you could, and the people who only ever loved the performance will go on being loved by it and will never know the difference and will never, ever come looking for you, because there is nothing out here to come looking for, only a person, aging, free, breathing the cold, with their own face their own again and no one left who recognises it.

You put your hands in your pockets. There is a folded snapshot in one, or a small true glass, or nothing at all but the cold, depending on what you kept of yourself coming down. You start to walk. The pavement goes somewhere this time. The corner you turn is where corners are supposed to be, and past it the street keeps going, indifferent, alive, and the marquee falls behind you and does not stay the same distance back; it recedes, the way real things recede, until it is one lit thing among many on a long ordinary street, and then it is around a corner, and then it is gone, and the applause is gone with it, and you walk on into a life where you will get older and be no one in particular and on some far night you will catch your reflection in a dark shop window and it will be just your face, on time, and you will not be sure, even then, which of you it was that walked out, only that one of you did, and is out here, in the cold, and is real.

There is no one to hold a door for you. You did not earn the Usher's latch and you did not stand and witness the child whole; you only knew, in your body, on the mark, that you would rather be no one real than the loved performance one more night. So you find the door yourself, by feel, along the cold back wall, the way you used to find your own face in a true glass in the dark.

It is there, low and plain, a fire door, a bar across it, a green sign half burned out. You set your hands on the bar. Behind you the spot finds the double on the empty mark and the house begins to let its breath go, the rain of it starting, for the part, not for you. You do not turn around to watch the version of yourself they prefer take the bow you spent a life learning to take.
The bar is cold. The cold beyond it is colder. You push.

choices.label: Go out, into the street, as yourself

blocks.s: You falter on the mark, between the doors, unable to choose, and into the holding silence comes a sound from the dark past the footlights you have heard at your back all night and never quite faced: a pencil, scratching, slow, on paper, and then a single pair of hands, coming together once, twice, deliberate, a slow clap that is not applause.

The Critic is the eldest in the house, the one who cast you at the need of the one you loved, the one who keeps what it finds worthy in the part forever. It does not raise its voice. It does not need to.

"You want me to decide," it says, dry, exact, almost kind for the length of one clause and then not. "You have stood there hoping someone will tell you which of the doors is the brave one, so that whichever you take, the choosing was not yours. I have reviewed a great many performances. I can give you a verdict. I can tell you, right now, which exit is the worthy one, and you will believe me, because you have wanted a critic's word your whole life more than you have wanted your own."

The pencil hovers. And you understand, far too late, that to take the verdict at all, from this voice, on this mark, is to be cast by it, kept by it, reviewed and filed and held in the part forever as a performance it judged. You wait for the word, hungry for it, as you have always been hungry for the word, and the word comes down, and it is favourable, and it keeps you.
